Key concepts: coordinates on the unit circle give (cos theta, sin theta), reference angles, quadrant signs (All Students Take Calculus mnemonic), special angles (0, 30, 45, 60, 90 degrees and their radian equivalents), and periodicity. Students should convert between degrees and radians (multiply by pi/180 or 180/pi). Tangent is defined as sin/cos. Vocabulary: unit circle, radian, reference angle, quadrantal angle, terminal side, standard position, coterminal angles, periodic. Common errors: confusing radian and degree mode, sign errors in quadrants II-IV, and misidentifying reference angles.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph functions including sine, cosine, rational, polynomial, and power functions and their transformations
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers graphing all six trigonometric functions for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2G). Students should graph sine, cosine, tangent, cotangent, secant, and cosecant, identifying key features. Key concepts: amplitude (|A|) and period (2pi/B for sin/cos, pi/B for tan/cot), vertical asymptotes for tan, cot, sec, csc, phase shifts, vertical shifts, and plotting key points per cycle. Students should graph at least two full periods showing maxima, minima, zeros, and asymptotes. Vocabulary: amplitude, period, frequency, phase shift, vertical shift, midline, asymptote, sinusoidal curve. Common errors: incorrect period calculation, misplacing asymptotes for tangent and cotangent, and confusing amplitude with range for shifted functions.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities: reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, cofunctions, even/odd, sum and difference
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers fundamental trigonometric identities for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5M). Students should verify and apply: reciprocal identities (csc = 1/sin, sec = 1/cos, cot = 1/tan), quotient identities (tan = sin/cos, cot = cos/sin), Pythagorean identities (sin^2+cos^2 = 1, 1+tan^2 = sec^2, 1+cot^2 = csc^2), cofunction identities, and even/odd identities (cos(-x) = cos(x), sin(-x) = -sin(x)). Key concepts: proving identities by transforming one side, strategic substitutions, factoring, and combining fractions. Vocabulary: identity, reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean identity, cofunction, even function, odd function. Common errors: treating identities as equations, working both sides simultaneously, and algebraic manipulation mistakes.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph arcsin x and arccos x and describe the limitations on the domain
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers arcsin, arccos, and arctan for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2H). Students should graph inverse trig functions and understand domain restrictions needed for invertibility. Key concepts: sin^(-1)(x) has domain [-1,1] and range [-pi/2, pi/2]; cos^(-1)(x) has domain [-1,1] and range [0, pi]; tan^(-1)(x) has domain all reals and range (-pi/2, pi/2). Students should evaluate compositions like sin(arccos(3/5)) and understand why restrictions are necessary. Vocabulary: inverse function, arcsine, arccosine, arctangent, restricted domain, principal value. Common errors: confusing sin^(-1)(x) with 1/sin(x), giving answers outside principal value range, and domain errors.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers solving equations involving trigonometric functions for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5N). Students should solve equations like 2sin(x) - 1 = 0 by isolating the trig function, finding reference angles, and determining all solutions within a given interval or using general solutions with period multiples. Key concepts: using inverse trig functions, finding solutions in all relevant quadrants, general solution notation (x = a + 2kpi), factoring trig equations, and using identities to simplify before solving. Vocabulary: general solution, particular solution, reference angle, quadrant, period, identity. Common errors: finding only one solution instead of all, not using identities to simplify, and losing solutions when dividing by trig functions.

Trigonometry.skill: Law of Sines and Law of Cosines
Trigonometry.teks_description: Use the Law of Sines in mathematical and real-world problems; P.4(H) Law of Cosines
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers solving oblique (non-right) triangles for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4G, P.4H). Law of Sines: a/sin(A) = b/sin(B) = c/sin(C). Law of Cosines: c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab*cos(C). Key concepts: when to use each law (Sines for AAS, ASA, SSA; Cosines for SAS, SSS), the ambiguous case (SSA may yield 0, 1, or 2 triangles), and finding area with A = (1/2)ab*sin(C). Vocabulary: oblique triangle, ambiguous case, included angle, solving a triangle, Law of Sines, Law of Cosines. Applications: surveying, navigation, indirect measurement. Common errors: forgetting the ambiguous case, using the wrong law, and calculator mode errors.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Describe the relationship between degree and radian measure on the unit circle
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers angle measurement systems for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4B). Students should convert between degrees and radians: radians = degrees * pi/180, degrees = radians * 180/pi. Key concepts: one radian is the angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius; a full circle = 2pi radians = 360 degrees; common radian values (pi/6, pi/4, pi/3, pi/2, pi, etc.); arc length s = r*theta (theta in radians); sector area A = (1/2)r^2*theta. Vocabulary: radian, degree, arc length, sector area, central angle, subtend, unit circle. Common errors: forgetting to use radians in arc length/sector area formulas, incorrect conversions, and mixing radian and degree values in calculations.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Use trigonometry in mathematical and real-world problems, including directional bearing
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers real-world applications of trigonometry for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4F). Students should use trig ratios, Law of Sines, and Law of Cosines to solve problems involving angles of elevation and depression, directional bearing, navigation, and indirect measurement. Key concepts: drawing diagrams from word descriptions, identifying right triangles within complex figures, using complementary angle relationships, and applying bearing notation (N30E, S45W). Vocabulary: angle of elevation, angle of depression, bearing, heading, line of sight, indirect measurement. Common errors: confusing elevation and depression angles, misinterpreting bearing directions, and not drawing accurate diagrams before solving.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph trigonometric functions and their transformations including amplitude, period, and phase shift
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers parameters of sinusoidal functions for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2G). For y = A*sin(B(x-C))+D: amplitude = |A| (half the vertical distance between max and min), period = 2pi/|B| (horizontal length of one cycle), phase shift = C (horizontal translation), vertical shift = D (midline). Key concepts: determining parameters from equations, writing equations from graphs, and understanding how each parameter transforms the parent function. Vocabulary: amplitude, period, frequency, phase shift, vertical shift, midline, sinusoidal. Common errors: forgetting absolute value for amplitude, incorrect period formula, wrong sign on phase shift, and confusing B with the period itself.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph trigonometric and power functions; represent complex numbers in trigonometric form
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers representing complex numbers in trigonometric form for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2F). Students should convert a + bi to r(cos theta + i*sin theta) where r = sqrt(a^2+b^2) and theta = arctan(b/a), adjusted for quadrant. Key concepts: modulus (r), argument (theta), multiplication in trig form (multiply moduli, add arguments), division (divide moduli, subtract arguments), and connection to polar coordinates. Vocabulary: complex number, modulus, argument, trigonometric form, polar form, rectangular form. Common errors: incorrect quadrant for argument, confusing modulus with absolute value of real part only, and arithmetic errors in conversion.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph points in the polar coordinate system and convert between rectangular and polar coordinates
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers the polar coordinate system for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.3D). Students should plot points (r, theta), convert between rectangular and polar coordinates using x = r*cos(theta), y = r*sin(theta), r = sqrt(x^2+y^2), theta = arctan(y/x). Key concepts: multiple representations of the same point, negative r values, graphing polar equations (circles, cardioids, rose curves, limacons, lemniscates), and converting equations between coordinate systems. Vocabulary: polar coordinates, pole, polar axis, cardioid, rose curve, limacon, lemniscate, spiral. Common errors: incorrect quadrant for theta, confusing (r,theta) plotting with (x,y), and not considering negative r.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Apply vector addition and multiplication of a vector by a scalar in mathematical and real-world problems
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers vector operations and applications for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4K). Students should add vectors graphically and algebraically, multiply vectors by scalars, find magnitude and direction, and apply vectors to real-world problems. Key concepts: component form <a,b>, magnitude ||v|| = sqrt(a^2+b^2), unit vectors, dot product (u dot v = |u||v|cos theta), and resolving vectors into components. Applications: force decomposition, velocity, displacement, navigation, work. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component, unit vector, dot product, resultant, equilibrium. Common errors: treating vectors as scalars, sign errors in components, and confusing direction angle with reference angle.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Use sum and difference identities for cosine and sine to simplify trigonometric expressions
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers sum and difference identities for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5M). Key formulas: sin(A+B) = sin(A)cos(B)+cos(A)sin(B), sin(A-B) = sin(A)cos(B)-cos(A)sin(B), cos(A+B) = cos(A)cos(B)-sin(A)sin(B), cos(A-B) = cos(A)cos(B)+sin(A)sin(B), tan(A+B) = (tan(A)+tan(B))/(1-tan(A)tan(B)). Key concepts: using these to find exact values of non-standard angles (e.g., sin(75) = sin(45+30)), simplifying expressions, and deriving other identities (double angle). Vocabulary: sum formula, difference formula, exact value, identity, derive. Common errors: sign errors in cosine sum/difference, misremembering which signs go where, and incorrect angle decomposition.

Trigonometry.skill: Double Angle and Half Angle Identities
Trigonometry.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities to simplify expressions including double and half angle formulas
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers double angle and half angle formulas for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5M). Double angle: sin(2A) = 2sin(A)cos(A), cos(2A) = cos^2(A)-sin^2(A) = 2cos^2(A)-1 = 1-2sin^2(A), tan(2A) = 2tan(A)/(1-tan^2(A)). Half angle: sin(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1-cos(A))/2), cos(A/2) = +/-sqrt((1+cos(A))/2). Key concepts: deriving from sum formulas, choosing the correct sign for half angle based on quadrant, solving equations using these identities, and power-reducing formulas. Vocabulary: double angle, half angle, power-reducing formula, identity, quadrant. Common errors: wrong sign choice for half angle, misapplying power-reducing forms, and confusing the three forms of cos(2A).

Trigonometry.teks_description: Use trigonometric identities including product-to-sum and sum-to-product formulas
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers product-to-sum and sum-to-product conversion formulas for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5M). Product-to-sum: sin(A)cos(B) = (1/2)[sin(A+B)+sin(A-B)], cos(A)cos(B) = (1/2)[cos(A-B)+cos(A+B)], sin(A)sin(B) = (1/2)[cos(A-B)-cos(A+B)]. Sum-to-product: sin(A)+sin(B) = 2sin((A+B)/2)cos((A-B)/2). Key concepts: simplifying products of trig functions into sums (useful in calculus integration), combining sums into products for solving equations, and applications in signal processing. Vocabulary: product-to-sum, sum-to-product, identity, simplify. Common errors: misremembering formulas, sign errors, and misidentifying when to use each direction of conversion.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Generate and solve trigonometric equations in mathematical and real-world problems
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers solving trigonometric equations and inequalities for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5N). Students should solve equations involving single and multiple trig functions, apply identities to reduce complexity, handle equations with multiple angles (e.g., sin(2x) = 1/2), and solve trig inequalities on specified intervals. Key concepts: general solutions using periodicity, substitution techniques for multiple-angle equations, factoring trig expressions, and graphical methods for inequalities. Vocabulary: trigonometric equation, general solution, particular solution, inequality, interval, period. Common errors: losing solutions by dividing by trig functions instead of factoring, not finding all solutions in the interval, and domain errors.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Represent angles in radians or degrees for problems including linear and angular velocity
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers angular and linear velocity for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4D). Students should relate angular velocity (omega = theta/t, in radians per unit time) to linear velocity (v = r*omega). Key concepts: distinguishing between angular measure (radians) and linear measure (arc length), converting RPM to radians per second, relating the velocities of gears or wheels of different radii, and applications to rotating machinery, satellites, and planetary motion. Vocabulary: angular velocity, linear velocity, radian, revolution, RPM, arc length, radius, period. Common errors: forgetting to convert degrees to radians, mixing up angular and linear velocity formulas, and unit conversion mistakes.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Develop and use a sinusoidal function that models harmonic motion situations
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers simple harmonic motion modeling for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2O). Students should model oscillating systems using d(t) = A*sin(omega*t + phi) or d(t) = A*cos(omega*t + phi). Key concepts: amplitude (maximum displacement), angular frequency (omega = 2pi/period), phase angle (initial condition), equilibrium position, and the relationship between period and frequency (f = 1/T). Applications: springs, pendulums, sound waves, electrical circuits. Vocabulary: simple harmonic motion, amplitude, period, frequency, angular frequency, phase angle, equilibrium, oscillation, damping. Common errors: confusing frequency and period, incorrect phase angle determination from initial conditions, and not using radians consistently.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Determine relationship between unit circle and periodic function to evaluate trigonometric functions
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers circular (wrapping) function interpretation for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4A). Students should understand how the unit circle defines sin and cos as functions of real numbers (not just angles), wrapping the real number line around the circle. Key concepts: the wrapping function, domain of circular functions (all reals for sin/cos), range [-1,1], periodicity (sin and cos have period 2pi, tan has period pi), and evaluating trig functions for any real input. Vocabulary: circular function, wrapping function, periodic, period, domain, range, unit circle. Applications: modeling cyclic phenomena. Common errors: restricting trig functions to acute angles only, not recognizing periodicity patterns, and confusing circular and right-triangle definitions.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Apply the Binomial Theorem for expansion of (a+b)^n in powers of a and b
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers De Moivre's Theorem and its applications for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.5F). The theorem states [r(cos theta + i*sin theta)]^n = r^n(cos(n*theta) + i*sin(n*theta)). Key concepts: raising complex numbers to integer powers, finding nth roots of complex numbers (n distinct roots equally spaced around a circle), roots of unity, and connection to the Binomial Theorem. Students should find all nth roots: r^(1/n)[cos((theta+2kpi)/n) + i*sin((theta+2kpi)/n)] for k = 0, 1, ..., n-1. Vocabulary: De Moivre's Theorem, nth root, root of unity, polar form, argument, modulus. Common errors: forgetting all n roots, incorrect angle division, and arithmetic errors with complex polar multiplication.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph functions and their transformations including translations, reflections, and stretches
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers transformations applied to trigonometric function graphs for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2G). Students should apply translations (horizontal and vertical shifts), reflections (over x-axis and y-axis), and stretches/compressions (vertical amplitude changes and horizontal period changes) to sin, cos, tan, and their reciprocals. Key concepts: writing equations from transformed graphs, identifying transformations from equations, combining multiple transformations, and understanding order of operations for transformations. Vocabulary: transformation, translation, reflection, stretch, compression, amplitude, period, phase shift, midline. Common errors: incorrect horizontal shift direction, applying vertical transformations to period, and order-of-transformation mistakes.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Develop and use a sinusoidal function that models a situation in real-world problems
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers using sinusoidal functions to model real-world periodic phenomena for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2O). Students should build models from data by determining amplitude (half the difference of max and min), vertical shift (average of max and min), period (time for one full cycle), and phase shift (alignment to start of cycle). Applications: tides, temperature, Ferris wheels, daylight hours, sound, electricity. Vocabulary: sinusoidal model, amplitude, period, phase shift, midline, fit, regression. Students should evaluate model accuracy and use models for prediction. Common errors: misidentifying period from data, confusing amplitude with maximum value, and incorrect B calculation (B = 2pi/period).

Trigonometry.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities as they relate to functions including trigonometric
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers limits involving trigonometric functions for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.2L). Students should evaluate special trig limits: lim(x->0) sin(x)/x = 1 and lim(x->0) (1-cos(x))/x = 0, identify discontinuities in trig functions (vertical asymptotes of tan, cot, sec, csc), and understand continuity of sin and cos on all reals. Key concepts: squeeze theorem intuition, behavior near asymptotes, one-sided limits, and connecting limit concepts to derivative preparation. Vocabulary: limit, continuity, discontinuity, asymptote, squeeze theorem, indeterminate form. Common errors: evaluating sin(x)/x at x=0 directly, not recognizing indeterminate forms, and mislocating asymptotes.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Use the Law of Sines and Law of Cosines to solve oblique triangles
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers comprehensive triangle solving for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.4G). Students should solve right triangles using SOH-CAH-TOA and the Pythagorean theorem, and oblique triangles using Law of Sines and Law of Cosines. Key concepts: choosing the appropriate method based on given information (AAS, ASA, SSA, SAS, SSS), the ambiguous case for SSA, finding all unknown sides and angles, and computing area. Vocabulary: right triangle, oblique triangle, solve a triangle, ambiguous case, angle of elevation, angle of depression. Applications: surveying, navigation, engineering. Common errors: using right-triangle methods on non-right triangles, forgetting the ambiguous case, and not finding all solutions.

Trigonometry.teks_description: Graph polar equations by plotting points and using technology
Trigonometry.prompt: This topic covers graphing polar equations and complex number representations for Trigonometry students (TEKS P.3E). Students should graph polar equations using technology and by plotting points, convert complex numbers between rectangular (a+bi) and polar (r*cis(theta)) forms, and perform operations in polar form. Key concepts: graphing rose curves (r = a*cos(n*theta)), limacons, cardioids, lemniscates, and spirals; symmetry tests for polar graphs; and multiplying/dividing complex numbers in polar form. Vocabulary: polar equation, polar graph, cis notation, symmetry, rose curve, limacon, cardioid, lemniscate. Common errors: incorrect symmetry analysis, missing petals in rose curves, and confusing multiplication rules in polar form.

Statistics And Probability.skill: Descriptive Statistics (Measures of Center and Spread)
Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Compare and contrast meaningful information derived from summary statistics given a data set
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers summary statistics for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.4E). Students should calculate and interpret mean, median, mode (center), and range, IQR, standard deviation, variance (spread). Key concepts: mean is sensitive to outliers; median is resistant; standard deviation measures typical distance from the mean; IQR captures the middle 50%. Students should compare distributions using these statistics and justify which measures are most appropriate for skewed vs. symmetric data. Vocabulary: mean, median, mode, range, IQR, standard deviation, variance, outlier, resistant measure, five-number summary, box plot. Common errors: using mean for heavily skewed data, miscalculating quartiles, and confusing population vs. sample standard deviation formulas.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Determine probabilities including the use of a two-way table
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers foundational probability theory for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5A). Students should understand Kolmogorov's axioms: P(E) >= 0 for all events; P(S) = 1 for the sample space; P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B) for mutually exclusive events. Key concepts: sample space, events, complement rule P(A') = 1-P(A), addition rule for non-mutually exclusive events P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B), two-way tables for organizing probabilities. Vocabulary: sample space, event, outcome, probability, complement, mutually exclusive, union, intersection, Venn diagram, two-way table. Common errors: double-counting in the addition rule, confusing mutually exclusive with independent, and probabilities exceeding 1.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Construct a distribution based on technology-generated simulation for a discrete random variable
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers random variables for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5C). Students should distinguish between discrete (countable outcomes) and continuous (measurable on an interval) random variables, construct probability distributions, and compute expected values and standard deviations. Key concepts: probability mass function (discrete), probability density function (continuous), cumulative distribution function, and the requirement that probabilities sum/integrate to 1. Vocabulary: random variable, discrete, continuous, probability distribution, probability mass function, density function, cumulative distribution. Common errors: treating continuous variables as discrete, probabilities not summing to 1, and confusing probability with frequency.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Construct a distribution for a discrete random variable based on simulation or collected samples
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers major probability distributions for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5C). Binomial: fixed n trials, two outcomes, P(X=k) = C(n,k)*p^k*(1-p)^(n-k). Poisson: models rare events, P(X=k) = (lambda^k * e^(-lambda))/k!. Normal: continuous bell curve defined by mean mu and standard deviation sigma, 68-95-99.7 empirical rule. Key concepts: identifying which distribution fits a scenario, computing probabilities using formulas and tables, normal approximation to binomial, and z-score standardization. Vocabulary: binomial, Poisson, normal, bell curve, parameters, z-score, standard normal. Common errors: misidentifying distribution type, using wrong parameters, and forgetting continuity correction.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Develop strategies using permutations and combinations
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers counting methods for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS G.13A). Permutations (order matters): nPr = n!/(n-r)!. Combinations (order does not matter): nCr = n!/((n-r)!r!). Key concepts: fundamental counting principle, factorial notation, distinguishing when order matters, counting with/without replacement, and applications to probability. Vocabulary: permutation, combination, factorial, fundamental counting principle, with replacement, without replacement, distinguishable, indistinguishable. Students should solve real-world counting problems and connect to probability calculations. Common errors: using permutations when combinations are needed, factorial arithmetic mistakes, and overcounting.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Determine probabilities including conditional probability using two-way tables
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers conditional probability for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5A). P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B). Bayes' Theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)*P(A)/P(B). Key concepts: two-way tables, tree diagrams, partition of sample space, total probability theorem P(B) = sum P(B|Ai)*P(Ai), and applications to medical testing (sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV). Vocabulary: conditional probability, Bayes' Theorem, prior, posterior, sensitivity, specificity, false positive, false negative, base rate. Common errors: confusing P(A|B) with P(B|A), ignoring base rates (base rate fallacy), and misinterpreting medical test results.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Compare statistical measures such as sample mean and standard deviation from sampling distributions
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers expected value and variance for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5D). Expected value E(X) = sum of x*P(x) for discrete variables. Variance Var(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 or sum of (x-mu)^2*P(x). Standard deviation = sqrt(Var(X)). Key concepts: E(X) as long-run average, linearity of expectation E(aX+b) = aE(X)+b, variance of linear transformations Var(aX+b) = a^2*Var(X), and comparing distributions using these measures. Vocabulary: expected value, variance, standard deviation, linear transformation, independent. Common errors: confusing expected value with most likely outcome, forgetting to square a in variance transformations, and mixing population and sample formulas.

Statistics And Probability.skill: Law of Large Numbers and Central Limit Theorem
Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Describe the relationship between theoretical and empirical probabilities using the Law of Large Numbers
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers foundational limit theorems for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5B). The Law of Large Numbers states that as sample size increases, the sample mean approaches the population mean. The Central Limit Theorem states that for large n, the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal with mean mu and standard deviation sigma/sqrt(n), regardless of population shape. Key concepts: sampling distribution, standard error, why CLT enables inference, and the n >= 30 guideline. Vocabulary: Law of Large Numbers, Central Limit Theorem, sampling distribution, standard error, convergence. Common errors: applying CLT with too-small samples, confusing standard deviation with standard error, and thinking CLT requires normal population.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Interpret p-values relative to significance levels to determine whether to reject the null hypothesis
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers hypothesis testing for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6H). Students should state null (H0) and alternative (Ha) hypotheses, calculate test statistics, determine p-values, and make decisions at a given significance level (alpha). Key concepts: Type I error (rejecting true H0), Type II error (failing to reject false H0), one-tailed vs. two-tailed tests, p-value as probability of observing data as extreme as or more extreme than sample given H0 is true. Vocabulary: null hypothesis, alternative hypothesis, test statistic, p-value, significance level, Type I error, Type II error, power, reject, fail to reject. Common errors: saying 'accept H0' instead of 'fail to reject,' interpreting p-value as P(H0 is true), and confusing statistical with practical significance.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Calculate confidence intervals for population means when normally distributed with known standard deviation
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers confidence intervals for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6C). For population mean with known sigma: CI = x-bar +/- z*(sigma/sqrt(n)). Key concepts: confidence level (percentage of intervals that contain the true parameter in repeated sampling), margin of error, relationship between confidence level and interval width, effect of sample size on precision, and conditions for valid inference (random sample, normality or large n). Vocabulary: confidence interval, margin of error, confidence level, point estimate, critical value, z-score, standard error. Common errors: interpreting confidence level as probability the parameter is in a specific interval, forgetting conditions, and confusing confidence level with probability.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Transform a linear parent function to determine a line of best fit
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers linear regression for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.7B). Students should find the line of best fit (least squares regression), interpret slope and intercept in context, and assess model fit. Key concepts: correlation coefficient r, coefficient of determination r^2, residuals, residual plots, influential points, and extrapolation dangers. Vocabulary: regression, slope, intercept, correlation, coefficient of determination, residual, residual plot, influential point, lurking variable, extrapolation. Students should distinguish between correlation and causation. Common errors: extrapolating far beyond data range, confusing r with r^2, treating regression as proving causation, and ignoring residual patterns.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Analyze hypothesis test outcomes using technology covering tests for proportions, means, and group differences
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers ANOVA for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6I). ANOVA tests whether means of three or more groups are equal by comparing within-group variability to between-group variability. Key concepts: F-statistic = MS_between/MS_within, null hypothesis (all group means are equal), assumptions (independent random samples, approximately normal populations, equal variances), and post-hoc tests for identifying which groups differ. Vocabulary: ANOVA, F-statistic, mean square, sum of squares, between-group, within-group, degrees of freedom, post-hoc test, Tukey's HSD. Common errors: using multiple t-tests instead of ANOVA (inflating Type I error), violating assumptions, and misinterpreting a significant F-test.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Analyze hypothesis test outcomes using technology-based statistical tests
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers chi-square tests for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6I). Goodness-of-fit tests whether observed frequencies match expected frequencies. Test of independence tests whether two categorical variables are associated using a contingency table. Key concepts: chi-square statistic = sum((observed-expected)^2/expected), degrees of freedom, expected cell counts (must be >= 5), and interpreting results. Vocabulary: chi-square, goodness-of-fit, test of independence, contingency table, observed frequency, expected frequency, degrees of freedom, categorical data. Common errors: using chi-square on quantitative data, expected counts too small, and confusing goodness-of-fit with independence tests.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Evaluate benefits of different sampling techniques including random and convenience sampling
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers sampling techniques for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.2A). Students should evaluate: simple random sampling (every subset equally likely), stratified sampling (divide into strata, sample from each), cluster sampling (randomly select whole clusters), systematic sampling (every kth element), and convenience sampling (non-random, biased). Key concepts: sampling frame, sampling bias, undercoverage, response bias, nonresponse bias, and how sampling method affects validity of inference. Vocabulary: population, sample, sampling frame, simple random sample, stratified, cluster, systematic, convenience, bias, representative. Common errors: confusing stratified with cluster sampling, assuming any sample is representative, and not recognizing sources of bias.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Compare statistical measures from simulated sampling distribution to theoretical sampling distribution
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers the standard normal distribution for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5D). Z-score = (x - mu)/sigma standardizes any normal distribution to mean 0, standard deviation 1. Key concepts: using z-tables or technology to find probabilities, finding values from given probabilities (inverse normal), comparing values from different distributions using z-scores, and the empirical rule (68-95-99.7). Vocabulary: standard normal, z-score, standardize, normal distribution, bell curve, percentile, empirical rule, probability density. Common errors: forgetting to subtract 1 for right-tail probabilities, using z-tables incorrectly, and applying normal calculations to non-normal data.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Analyze hypothesis test outcomes using technology for proportions, means, and differences
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers t-distributions for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6I). When population sigma is unknown, use t-distribution with df = n-1. Key concepts: t-distribution is bell-shaped but with heavier tails than normal, approaches normal as df increases, one-sample t-test and t-interval for population mean, paired t-test for matched pairs, two-sample t-procedures for comparing means. Vocabulary: t-distribution, degrees of freedom, t-statistic, t-interval, one-sample, two-sample, paired, pooled, Welch's approximation. Common errors: using z instead of t when sigma is unknown, incorrect degrees of freedom, and violating normality assumption with small samples without checking.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Analyze hypothesis test outcomes using technology including F-distribution based tests
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers the F-distribution for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6I). The F-distribution is used in ANOVA and for comparing two population variances. F = s1^2/s2^2 follows an F-distribution with (n1-1, n2-1) degrees of freedom under H0 of equal variances. Key concepts: F-distribution is right-skewed and non-negative, depends on two df parameters (numerator and denominator), connection to ANOVA F-tests, and Levene's test for equal variances. Vocabulary: F-distribution, F-statistic, numerator degrees of freedom, denominator degrees of freedom, variance ratio, homogeneity of variances, Levene's test. Common errors: swapping numerator and denominator df, applying F-test to non-normal data, and confusing F-test in ANOVA with F-test for equal variances.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Analyze hypothesis test outcomes using technology-based statistical tests
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers nonparametric statistical tests for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6I). Nonparametric tests do not assume a specific population distribution. Key tests: Wilcoxon signed-rank (paired data), Mann-Whitney U (two independent samples), Kruskal-Wallis (k independent samples), Spearman rank correlation, and sign test. Key concepts: when to use nonparametric vs. parametric tests (small samples, ordinal data, non-normal distributions, outliers), ranking data, and reduced statistical power compared to parametric alternatives. Vocabulary: nonparametric, rank, distribution-free, Wilcoxon, Mann-Whitney, Kruskal-Wallis, Spearman, ordinal, power. Common errors: defaulting to parametric tests with violated assumptions and misinterpreting rank-based results.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Construct distributions based on technology-generated simulations
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers probability generating functions (PGFs) for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5C). The PGF of a discrete random variable X is G(s) = E(s^X) = sum P(X=k)*s^k. Key concepts: G(1) = 1, G'(1) = E(X), G''(1) = E(X(X-1)), recovering probabilities from derivatives (P(X=k) = G^(k)(0)/k!), PGF of sum of independent variables is product of their PGFs, and applications to branching processes. Vocabulary: probability generating function, moment generating function, convolution, independent, expected value, factorial moment. Common errors: confusing PGF with moment generating function, convergence issues, and differentiation errors.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Compare statistical measures from simulated sampling distributions (extends beyond standard TEKS)
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic introduces Markov chains for Statistics and Probability students (extends TEKS S.5D). A Markov chain is a sequence of random variables where the future state depends only on the current state (memoryless property). Key concepts: state space, transition matrix, transition probabilities, n-step transition matrix (P^n), stationary/steady-state distribution (pi*P = pi), absorbing states, and regular vs. absorbing chains. Vocabulary: Markov chain, state, transition probability, transition matrix, stationary distribution, steady state, absorbing state, irreducible, aperiodic. Applications: weather modeling, random walks, PageRank. Common errors: violating the Markov property assumption, matrix multiplication errors, and confusing transient with absorbing states.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Construct distributions based on technology-generated simulations
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic introduces Monte Carlo simulation methods for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5C). Monte Carlo methods use random sampling to estimate mathematical quantities or model complex systems. Key concepts: generating random numbers, simulating random processes, estimating probabilities through repeated trials, estimating integrals and areas, convergence and accuracy (error proportional to 1/sqrt(n)), and variance reduction techniques. Vocabulary: Monte Carlo, simulation, random number generator, trial, convergence, variance reduction, bootstrap, resampling. Applications: estimating pi, option pricing, risk analysis. Common errors: insufficient number of trials, poor random number generation, and misinterpreting simulation results as exact values.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Describe and model variability using population and sampling distributions
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers rules for data spread for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.3D). The Empirical Rule (for normal distributions): approximately 68% of data within 1 SD, 95% within 2 SD, 99.7% within 3 SD of the mean. Chebyshev's Theorem (for any distribution): at least 1-1/k^2 of data lies within k standard deviations of the mean (k > 1). Key concepts: when to apply each rule (Empirical requires normality; Chebyshev applies universally), identifying unusual observations, and connecting to z-scores. Vocabulary: Empirical Rule, Chebyshev's Theorem, standard deviation, normal distribution, bell-shaped, unusual value. Common errors: applying the Empirical Rule to non-normal data, miscomputing Chebyshev's fraction, and confusing the two rules.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Distinguish among different sources of variability including measurement, natural, induced, and sampling
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers how operations on random variables affect their distributions for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.3C). Key results: E(aX+b) = aE(X)+b, Var(aX+b) = a^2*Var(X), SD(aX+b) = |a|*SD(X). For independent variables: E(X+Y) = E(X)+E(Y), Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y). Key concepts: linear transformations, combining independent random variables, standardization as a special transformation, and sources of variability (measurement, natural, induced, sampling). Vocabulary: transformation, linear combination, independence, additive, expected value, variance, standard deviation. Common errors: adding variances of dependent variables, forgetting to square the constant for variance, and confusing SD addition rules.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Describe how sample statistics serve as evidence contradicting claims about population parameters
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers drawing conclusions from data for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6F). Students should understand how sample statistics provide evidence about population parameters and make informed decisions using confidence intervals and hypothesis tests. Key concepts: point estimates, sampling distributions, margins of error, significance levels, power analysis, and communicating statistical findings clearly. Vocabulary: inference, parameter, statistic, point estimate, interval estimate, significance, power, decision, evidence, conclusion. Students should evaluate claims using data and communicate uncertainty. Common errors: confusing sample statistics with population parameters, overstating conclusions from non-significant results, and ignoring practical significance.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Explain how adjustments to sample size, confidence level, and standard deviation influence margin of error
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers statistical power and sample size determination for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.6B). Power = P(rejecting H0 when H0 is false) = 1 - P(Type II error). Key concepts: factors affecting power (sample size, significance level, effect size, variability), how increasing n increases power, trade-offs between alpha and beta, calculating required sample size for desired power and margin of error. Vocabulary: power, Type II error, beta, effect size, sample size, margin of error, significance level, alpha, detectable difference. Common errors: confusing power with significance level, not considering effect size, and using inadequate sample sizes for detecting meaningful differences.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Construct distributions based on technology-generated simulations or collected samples
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic covers bootstrap resampling for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.5C). The bootstrap estimates the sampling distribution of a statistic by resampling with replacement from the original sample. Key concepts: resampling with replacement, generating bootstrap distribution, constructing bootstrap confidence intervals (percentile method, BCa), estimating standard errors without formulas, and when bootstrap is appropriate. Vocabulary: bootstrap, resampling, with replacement, bootstrap distribution, percentile interval, standard error, nonparametric. Applications: estimating CI for median, correlation, regression coefficients. Common errors: resampling without replacement (that's permutation), too few bootstrap samples, and applying to very small original samples.

Statistics And Probability.teks_description: Examine scatterplots to identify patterns, linearity, outliers, and influential points
Statistics And Probability.prompt: This topic introduces time series analysis for Statistics and Probability students (TEKS S.7A). Students should examine sequential data for patterns including trend, seasonality, and random variation. Key concepts: time plot, trend (long-term direction), seasonal component (repeating pattern within fixed periods), cyclical component, decomposition (additive vs. multiplicative), moving averages for smoothing, and autocorrelation. Vocabulary: time series, trend, seasonal, cyclical, residual, moving average, autocorrelation, decomposition, stationary, forecast. Common errors: confusing seasonal and cyclical patterns, not accounting for trend when analyzing seasonality, and overfitting short time series.

College Algebra.teks_description: Graph functions including square root, reciprocal, cubic, cube root, exponential, absolute value, and logarithmic
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers graphing and analyzing major function families for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.2A). Students should graph square root, reciprocal, cubic, cube root, exponential, absolute value, and logarithmic functions. Key concepts: domain and range of each family, identifying key features (intercepts, asymptotes, extrema), continuity, end behavior, and relating algebraic form to graphical shape. Students should determine domain restrictions (square root requires non-negative radicand, log requires positive argument, reciprocal excludes zero denominator). Vocabulary: function family, parent function, domain, range, intercept, asymptote, end behavior, continuous, piecewise. Common errors: forgetting domain restrictions, misidentifying end behavior, and confusing function families.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine the linear factors of a polynomial function of degree three and four
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers analyzing polynomial functions of degree three and four for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.7D). Students should find linear factors using Rational Root Theorem, synthetic division, and factoring techniques, then sketch graphs. Key concepts: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, relationship between factors and zeros, multiplicity effects on graphs, end behavior from leading term, turning points (at most n-1 for degree n), and finding rational zeros. Vocabulary: polynomial, degree, zero, root, factor, multiplicity, turning point, Rational Root Theorem, Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, leading coefficient test. Common errors: missing zeros with multiplicity, incorrect end behavior, and not testing all rational root candidates.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers transformation effects on exponential and logarithmic graphs for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.5A). Students should graph f(x) = a*b^(x-h)+k and f(x) = a*log_b(x-h)+k, identifying transformations from parent functions. Key concepts: horizontal asymptote for exponential (y = k), vertical asymptote for logarithmic (x = h), domain/range relationships, reflections, stretches, and the inverse relationship between exponential and logarithmic functions. Vocabulary: exponential function, logarithmic function, asymptote, transformation, base, natural log, common log, inverse functions. Common errors: confusing domain of log with range, misplacing asymptotes after transformations, and forgetting that log is undefined for non-positive inputs.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve algebraically systems of two equations consisting of a linear and a quadratic equation
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers solving systems of linear and nonlinear equations for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.3C). Students should solve systems algebraically (substitution, elimination) and graphically, handling combinations of linear, quadratic, rational, and exponential equations. Key concepts: interpreting solutions as intersection points, 0/1/2/infinite solution cases, using matrices for linear systems, and nonlinear system strategies (substitution is primary). Vocabulary: system of equations, substitution, elimination, intersection, consistent, inconsistent, linear, nonlinear, solution set. Common errors: algebraic errors during substitution, forgetting to solve for all variables, and misinterpreting graphical intersections.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve three-variable linear systems using Gaussian elimination, matrices, or substitution
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers matrix methods for solving systems for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.3B). Students should solve 3-variable systems using Gaussian elimination, represent systems as augmented matrices, perform row operations, compute 2x2 and 3x3 determinants, and apply Cramer's rule. Key concepts: row echelon and reduced row echelon form, elementary row operations, matrix notation, singular vs. nonsingular matrices, and interpreting determinant = 0 as no unique solution. Vocabulary: matrix, augmented matrix, row echelon form, RREF, determinant, Gaussian elimination, Cramer's rule, pivot, singular. Common errors: row operation arithmetic mistakes, determinant cofactor expansion errors, and misapplying Cramer's rule to singular systems.

College Algebra.teks_description: Add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers complex number operations for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.7A). Students should add, subtract, and multiply complex numbers in the form a+bi, where i = sqrt(-1) and i^2 = -1. Key concepts: real and imaginary parts, complex conjugates, dividing by multiplying by the conjugate, plotting on the complex plane, modulus |a+bi| = sqrt(a^2+b^2), and connecting to quadratic equations with negative discriminant. Vocabulary: complex number, imaginary unit, real part, imaginary part, complex conjugate, modulus, complex plane, Argand diagram. Common errors: forgetting i^2 = -1 when multiplying, not rationalizing denominators, and treating i as a real variable.

College Algebra.teks_description: Represent arithmetic and geometric series using sigma notation
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers arithmetic and geometric sequences and series for College Algebra students (TEKS P.5D). Students should write sequences in explicit and recursive form, express series in sigma notation, and compute partial sums. Key formulas: arithmetic sum S_n = n(a_1+a_n)/2, geometric sum S_n = a_1(1-r^n)/(1-r), infinite geometric sum S = a_1/(1-r) when |r| < 1. Key concepts: identifying common difference or ratio, finding specific terms, determining convergence of infinite geometric series. Vocabulary: sequence, series, sigma notation, arithmetic, geometric, common difference, common ratio, convergent, divergent, partial sum. Common errors: index errors, misapplying sum formulas, and not checking convergence conditions.

College Algebra.teks_description: Make connections between locus definition of conic sections and their equations in rectangular coordinates
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers equations and properties of conic sections for College Algebra students (TEKS P.3G). Students should identify, classify, and graph circles, ellipses, parabolas, and hyperbolas from their equations, convert between general and standard forms (completing the square), and connect the locus definition to algebraic equations. Key properties: circle (center, radius), ellipse (center, vertices, foci, major/minor axes), parabola (vertex, focus, directrix), hyperbola (center, vertices, foci, asymptotes). Vocabulary: conic section, locus, eccentricity, focus, directrix, vertex, center, major axis, minor axis, asymptote, degenerate conic. Common errors: misclassifying conics, completing the square errors, and confusing a and b in ellipse/hyperbola.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve absolute value linear inequalities
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers solving absolute value inequalities for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.6F). Students should solve |ax+b| < c (compound inequality: -c < ax+b < c), |ax+b| > c (disjunction: ax+b < -c or ax+b > c), and graph solutions on number lines and in interval notation. Key concepts: absolute value as distance, equivalent compound inequalities, testing intervals for polynomial and rational inequalities, and sign analysis. Vocabulary: absolute value, inequality, compound inequality, conjunction, disjunction, interval notation, sign chart, test point, critical value. Common errors: forgetting to reverse inequality when multiplying by negative, wrong compound inequality direction, and not checking extraneous solutions.

College Algebra.teks_description: Apply the Binomial Theorem for expansion of (a+b)^n in powers of a and b
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Binomial Theorem for College Algebra students (TEKS P.5F). The theorem states (a+b)^n = sum_{k=0}^{n} C(n,k)*a^(n-k)*b^k. Students should expand binomials, find specific terms, and connect to Pascal's triangle. Key concepts: binomial coefficients C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n-k)!), Pascal's triangle patterns, finding the (r+1)th term, and applications. Vocabulary: Binomial Theorem, binomial coefficient, Pascal's triangle, expansion, factorial, combination, term. Common errors: off-by-one errors in term identification, forgetting to apply exponents to both coefficient and variable, and sign errors with subtraction (a-b)^n.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve rational equations that have real solutions including partial fraction decomposition
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers partial fraction decomposition for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.6I). Students should decompose rational expressions into simpler fractions: linear factors yield A/(ax+b), repeated linear factors yield A/(ax+b) + B/(ax+b)^2, and irreducible quadratic factors yield (Ax+B)/(ax^2+bx+c). Key concepts: proper vs. improper fractions (perform long division first if improper), setting up the decomposition based on denominator factoring, solving for coefficients using substitution or equating coefficients. Vocabulary: partial fraction, decomposition, proper fraction, improper fraction, linear factor, irreducible quadratic, coefficient. Common errors: incorrect factoring of denominator, missing repeated factor terms, and algebraic errors when solving for coefficients.

College Algebra.teks_description: Graph functions and analyze domain/range including transformations
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers function transformations for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.2A). Students should apply vertical/horizontal shifts, reflections, stretches/compressions to all major function families. Key concepts: for y = af(b(x-h))+k, a controls vertical stretch/reflection, b controls horizontal stretch/reflection, h controls horizontal shift, k controls vertical shift. Students should write equations from transformed graphs and identify transformations from equations. Vocabulary: parent function, transformation, translation, reflection, stretch, compression, scale factor. Common errors: confusion about horizontal shift direction (f(x-h) shifts right), mixing up vertical and horizontal effects, and incorrect order of transformations.

College Algebra.teks_description: Graph and write the inverse of a function using notation such as f^-1(x)
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers inverse functions for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.2B). Students should find inverses algebraically (swap x and y, solve for y), verify using composition (f(f^(-1)(x)) = x and f^(-1)(f(x)) = x), graph inverses as reflections over y = x, and determine when inverses exist. Key concepts: one-to-one functions (horizontal line test), domain restrictions for invertibility, inverse notation f^(-1)(x), and the relationship between domain/range of a function and its inverse. Vocabulary: inverse function, one-to-one, horizontal line test, composition, reflection, domain restriction. Common errors: confusing f^(-1)(x) with 1/f(x), not restricting domains, and algebraic errors when solving for y.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine various types of discontinuities as they relate to functions
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the concept of limits and continuity for College Algebra students (TEKS P.2L). Students should evaluate limits graphically and algebraically, identify types of discontinuities (removable, jump, infinite), and understand continuity requirements. Key concepts: limit exists when left-hand and right-hand limits agree, direct substitution for continuous functions, factoring and rationalizing to resolve 0/0 indeterminate forms, and limits at infinity for end behavior. Vocabulary: limit, continuity, discontinuity, removable, jump, infinite, one-sided limit, indeterminate form, end behavior. Common errors: confusing function value with limit, assuming limit equals function value at discontinuities, and not checking both sides.

College Algebra.teks_description: Use the composition of two functions to model and solve real-world problems
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers function composition and combined operations for College Algebra students (TEKS P.2A). Students should perform (f+g)(x), (f-g)(x), (fg)(x), (f/g)(x), and (f o g)(x) = f(g(x)), determine domains of composite functions, and model real-world situations using composition. Key concepts: domain of f o g requires x in domain of g AND g(x) in domain of f, decomposing complex functions into compositions, and evaluating compositions numerically and algebraically. Vocabulary: composition, composite function, domain, operation, decompose, inner function, outer function. Common errors: evaluating composition in wrong order (f o g is not g o f), incorrect domain determination, and algebraic simplification errors.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve polynomial equations with real coefficients by applying a variety of techniques
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers solving polynomial equations for College Algebra students (TEKS P.5J). Students should solve polynomial equations using factoring, Rational Root Theorem, synthetic division, grouping, substitution (for biquadratic equations like x^4-5x^2+4 = 0), and technology. Key concepts: Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, Descartes' Rule of Signs (counting positive/negative real roots), upper/lower bound theorems, and complex roots occur in conjugate pairs for real-coefficient polynomials. Vocabulary: polynomial equation, Rational Root Theorem, synthetic division, Descartes' Rule, conjugate pairs, depressed polynomial. Common errors: missing roots, forgetting complex conjugate pairs, and incorrect application of Descartes' Rule.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine linear and quadratic factors including sum/difference of cubes and grouping
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Rational Root Theorem for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.7E). If p/q (in lowest terms) is a rational root of a polynomial with integer coefficients, then p divides the constant term and q divides the leading coefficient. Key concepts: listing all possible rational roots, testing candidates using synthetic division, reducing the polynomial degree after finding roots, and recognizing when all rational candidates fail (irrational or complex roots). Vocabulary: Rational Root Theorem, possible rational roots, synthetic division, depressed polynomial, factor, zero. Students should combine with factoring by grouping and sum/difference of cubes. Common errors: missing negative candidates, not reducing p/q to lowest terms, and stopping after finding one root.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic polynomials
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers synthetic division for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.7C). Students should divide polynomials by (x-c) using synthetic division, interpret the remainder (Remainder Theorem: remainder = f(c)), and apply the Factor Theorem (if f(c) = 0, then (x-c) is a factor). Key concepts: setting up synthetic division correctly, including zero coefficients for missing terms, reading quotient coefficients, and using synthetic division iteratively to completely factor polynomials. Vocabulary: synthetic division, divisor, quotient, remainder, Remainder Theorem, Factor Theorem, depressed polynomial. Common errors: forgetting zero placeholders for missing degree terms, using the wrong sign for c, and misreading the quotient degree.

College Algebra.teks_description: Formulate exponential and logarithmic equations that model real-world situations
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers exponential and logarithmic modeling for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.5B). Students should write and solve equations for exponential growth y = a*e^(kt) or y = a*b^t and decay, including compound interest A = P(1+r/n)^(nt), continuous growth/decay A = Pe^(rt), half-life, and doubling time. Key concepts: identifying growth (k>0, b>1) vs. decay (k<0, 0<b<1), using logarithms to solve for time, fitting exponential models to data, and comparing growth rates. Vocabulary: exponential growth, exponential decay, half-life, doubling time, continuous compounding, growth factor, decay factor, initial value. Common errors: confusing rate with factor, logarithm errors when solving for t, and not matching time units.

College Algebra.teks_description: Graph piecewise defined functions and analyze their properties
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers piecewise-defined functions for College Algebra students (TEKS P.2F). Students should graph piecewise functions, determine domain and range, evaluate at boundary and non-boundary points, and analyze continuity. Key concepts: each piece is defined on a specific interval, graphing with open/closed endpoints, determining if the function is continuous at boundaries (left limit = right limit = function value), and real-world applications (tax brackets, shipping costs, utility pricing). Vocabulary: piecewise function, interval, boundary point, continuous, discontinuous, open endpoint, closed endpoint, step function. Common errors: using wrong formula at boundaries, graphing incorrect endpoints, and missing discontinuities.

College Algebra.teks_description: Graph a set of parametric equations
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers parametric equations for College Algebra students (TEKS P.3A). Students should graph sets of parametric equations x = f(t), y = g(t), eliminate the parameter to find rectangular equations, and understand the role of the parameter as a third variable (often time). Key concepts: plotting by creating a table of t, x, y values; eliminating t by solving one equation and substituting; direction of motion (orientation); and restricted domains in rectangular form. Vocabulary: parametric equations, parameter, eliminate, rectangular form, orientation, trajectory, curve. Common errors: losing domain restrictions when eliminating t, incorrect orientation, and algebra errors during elimination.

College Algebra.teks_description: Use vectors to model situations involving magnitude and direction
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers vector fundamentals for College Algebra students (TEKS P.4I). Students should represent vectors in component form and as directed line segments, perform vector addition, scalar multiplication, and find magnitude and direction. Key concepts: magnitude ||v|| = sqrt(a^2+b^2), direction angle theta = arctan(b/a), unit vectors (v/||v||), standard unit vectors i and j, and applications to force and velocity. Vocabulary: vector, scalar, magnitude, direction, component form, unit vector, resultant, equilibrium. Common errors: confusing vectors with scalars, direction angle quadrant errors, and component sign mistakes.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine the value of trigonometric ratios and solve problems involving trigonometric ratios
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers trigonometric functions as preparation for College Algebra students (TEKS P.4E). Students should evaluate all six trig functions using right triangles and the unit circle, solve basic trig equations, and understand periodicity. Key concepts: SOH-CAH-TOA, reciprocal relationships (csc = 1/sin, etc.), special angle values (30-45-60), reference angles, quadrant sign analysis, and inverse trig functions for finding angles. Vocabulary: sine, cosine, tangent, cosecant, secant, cotangent, unit circle, reference angle, period, inverse trig function. Common errors: calculator mode confusion (degrees vs. radians), mislabeling triangle sides, and not finding all solutions to equations.

College Algebra.teks_description: Analyze situations modeled by functions to solve real-world problems
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers using functions to model real-world situations for College Algebra students (TEKS P.2N). Students should select appropriate function types (linear, quadratic, polynomial, rational, exponential, logarithmic), determine parameters from context, and use models for analysis and prediction. Key concepts: defining variables and constraints, evaluating model fit, domain restrictions from context, interpreting parameters and results, and understanding limitations of mathematical models. Vocabulary: model, parameter, constraint, domain restriction, prediction, interpolation, extrapolation, optimization. Common errors: choosing inappropriate function types, extrapolating beyond valid range, and ignoring contextual constraints.

College Algebra.teks_description: Apply mathematics to problems arising in everyday life, society, and the workplace
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers mathematical problem-solving strategies for College Algebra students (TEKS A.1A). Students should apply algebraic techniques to real-world problems including optimization, mixture problems, work problems, and financial mathematics. Key concepts: defining variables, writing equations from word descriptions, solving and interpreting in context, and checking reasonableness. Vocabulary: optimize, maximize, minimize, constraint, system, mixture, rate, work, investment, break-even. Students should communicate solutions clearly with appropriate units and contextual interpretation. Common errors: poorly defined variables, incorrect equation setup, and solutions that don't make sense in context.

College Algebra.teks_description: Determine quotients of polynomials divided by linear or quadratic polynomials
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers dividing polynomials for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.7C). Students should perform long division and synthetic division of polynomials, interpret results using the Division Algorithm (dividend = divisor * quotient + remainder), and apply the Remainder and Factor Theorems. Key concepts: long division process mirrors numerical long division, synthetic division is efficient for divisors of the form (x-c), connecting division to factoring, and using division to reduce polynomial degree. Vocabulary: dividend, divisor, quotient, remainder, Division Algorithm, Remainder Theorem, Factor Theorem, synthetic division, long division. Common errors: zero-placeholder omission, subtraction sign errors in long division, and incorrect setup of synthetic division.

College Algebra.teks_description: Solve systems of two or more linear inequalities in two variables
College Algebra.prompt: This topic covers solving systems of linear inequalities for College Algebra students (TEKS 2A.3F). Students should graph individual linear inequalities (dashed line for strict, solid for inclusive, shading test-point side), find the feasible region as the intersection of all constraint regions, and identify vertices. Key concepts: half-planes, feasible region, corner points (vertices), linear programming connection (optimal values occur at vertices), and bounded vs. unbounded regions. Vocabulary: system of inequalities, half-plane, feasible region, vertex, bounded, unbounded, constraint, linear programming, test point. Common errors: incorrect shading direction, using wrong line style, and missing intersection points.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.4(I) vectors and P.3(A) parametric equations
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers vectors in 2D and 3D and vector-valued functions for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.1). Students should work with vectors in R^2 and R^3: addition, scalar multiplication, dot product, cross product, and vector-valued functions r(t) = <f(t), g(t), h(t)>. Key concepts: magnitude, unit vectors, direction cosines, parametric curves in space, velocity and acceleration vectors, derivatives and integrals of vector functions, arc length parameterization. Vocabulary: vector-valued function, dot product, cross product, parametric curve, tangent vector, velocity, acceleration, arc length parameterization. Common errors: cross product order matters (non-commutative), confusing dot and cross product, and forgetting vector function derivatives are component-wise.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.2(F) graphing multiple function types
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers functions of two or more variables for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.2). Students should graph surfaces in 3D using level curves (contour maps), traces (cross-sections with coordinate planes), and technology. Key surfaces: planes, spheres, cylinders, cones, paraboloids, hyperboloids, and ellipsoids. Key concepts: domain as a region in R^2 or R^3, range as subset of R, level curves f(x,y) = c, and level surfaces f(x,y,z) = c. Vocabulary: multivariable function, level curve, contour map, trace, surface, domain, range, quadric surface. Common errors: misinterpreting level curves, confusing function value with distance, and sketching 3D surfaces incorrectly.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.2(I) analyzing key features of functions
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers partial differentiation for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.3). Students should compute partial derivatives f_x and f_y by differentiating with respect to one variable while holding others constant, compute higher-order partials (f_xx, f_xy, f_yy), and form the gradient vector nabla f = <f_x, f_y, f_z>. Key concepts: gradient points in direction of steepest ascent, Clairaut's theorem (f_xy = f_yx for continuous second partials), and the gradient's magnitude gives the maximum rate of change. Vocabulary: partial derivative, gradient, nabla, mixed partial, Clairaut's theorem, rate of change, steepest ascent. Common errors: not treating other variables as constants, mixed partial order errors, and misinterpreting gradient direction.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.5(A) evaluating finite sums
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers iterated integration for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.4). Students should evaluate double integrals over rectangular and non-rectangular regions using iterated integration, choose appropriate order of integration, and extend to triple integrals. Key concepts: Fubini's theorem, Type I and Type II regions, setting up integration bounds from region descriptions, and applications (area, volume, average value). Vocabulary: double integral, triple integral, iterated integral, Fubini's theorem, region of integration, order of integration, Type I/II region. Common errors: incorrect integration bounds, wrong order of integration, and not sketching the region before setting up integrals.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; advanced calculus topic
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers integration along curves and over surfaces for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.5). Students should evaluate scalar line integrals (integral of f ds along curve C), vector line integrals (integral of F dot dr for work), scalar surface integrals (integral of f dS), and vector surface integrals (flux). Key concepts: parameterizing curves and surfaces, ds = ||r'(t)|| dt, work = integral F dot dr, flux = integral F dot n dS, and orientation. Vocabulary: line integral, surface integral, work, flux, parameterization, orientation, scalar field, vector field. Common errors: incorrect parameterization, forgetting the magnitude of r'(t), and orientation sign errors.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.4(K) vector applications
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers vector fields for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.6). Students should sketch and interpret vector fields F(x,y) = <P(x,y), Q(x,y)>, understand flow lines (integral curves), and identify properties like source, sink, and circulation. Key concepts: velocity fields, force fields, gradient fields (conservative), plotting vector fields by evaluating at sample points, and streamlines as tangent to the field at every point. Vocabulary: vector field, flow line, streamline, source, sink, conservative field, gradient field, circulation. Applications: fluid flow, electric/magnetic fields, gravitational fields. Common errors: confusing flow lines with level curves, misidentifying conservative fields, and direction errors in plotting.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; advanced vector calculus
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers differential operators on vector fields for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.7). Divergence: div F = nabla dot F = dP/dx + dQ/dy + dR/dz (scalar output, measures source/sink strength). Curl: curl F = nabla x F (vector output, measures rotation tendency). Key concepts: divergence-free fields (incompressible), curl-free fields (irrotational/conservative), and the relationships curl(grad f) = 0 and div(curl F) = 0. Vocabulary: divergence, curl, irrotational, incompressible, solenoidal, conservative, source, sink, vorticity. Common errors: computing curl with wrong sign pattern, confusing divergence (scalar) and curl (vector), and misinterpreting physical meaning.

Multivariable Calculus.skill: Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, and Divergence Theorem
Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; fundamental theorems of vector calculus
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers the fundamental theorems of vector calculus for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.8). Green's Theorem: relates a line integral around a simple closed curve to a double integral over the enclosed region. Stokes' Theorem: relates a surface integral of curl F to a line integral around the boundary. Divergence Theorem: relates a surface integral of flux to a triple integral of divergence. Key concepts: orientation conventions, when each theorem applies, simplifying calculations by choosing the easier side. Vocabulary: Green's Theorem, Stokes' Theorem, Divergence Theorem, boundary, orientation, closed surface, flux, circulation. Common errors: wrong orientation, applying theorems when hypotheses aren't met, and setup errors.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.3(A) parametric equations
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers parametric representations of surfaces for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.9). A parametric surface is r(u,v) = <x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v)>. Surface area = double integral of ||r_u x r_v|| dA. Key concepts: parameterizing common surfaces (spheres, cylinders, cones, graphs z = f(x,y)), tangent planes from r_u x r_v, normal vectors, and computing surface area using the cross product magnitude. Vocabulary: parametric surface, parameter domain, tangent plane, normal vector, surface area element, cross product. Common errors: incorrect parameterization bounds, cross product errors, and forgetting the magnitude when computing surface area.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.3(D) polar coordinates
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers multiple coordinate systems for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.10). Cylindrical: (r, theta, z) where x = r*cos(theta), y = r*sin(theta), z = z; volume element dV = r dr d(theta) dz. Spherical: (rho, phi, theta) where x = rho*sin(phi)*cos(theta), y = rho*sin(phi)*sin(theta), z = rho*cos(phi); volume element dV = rho^2*sin(phi) d(rho) d(phi) d(theta). Key concepts: choosing the best coordinate system based on symmetry, converting between systems, and adjusting integration bounds. Vocabulary: cylindrical, spherical, Cartesian, coordinate transformation, Jacobian, volume element. Common errors: confusing phi and theta conventions, forgetting the Jacobian factors, and incorrect bounds.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; constrained optimization
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers constrained optimization for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.11). To optimize f(x,y,z) subject to constraint g(x,y,z) = c, solve nabla f = lambda * nabla g and g = c simultaneously. Key concepts: the geometric insight (gradients are parallel at extrema), multiple constraints (two Lagrange multipliers), second-order conditions, comparing critical values to find absolute extrema, and unconstrained optimization using critical points and the Second Derivative Test. Vocabulary: Lagrange multiplier, constraint, optimization, critical point, saddle point, Second Derivative Test, Hessian. Common errors: forgetting the constraint equation, not finding all solutions, and not checking boundary cases.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; multivariable chain rule
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers the multivariable chain rule for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.12). If z = f(x,y) where x = g(t), y = h(t), then dz/dt = (df/dx)(dx/dt) + (df/dy)(dy/dt). For functions of multiple intermediate variables, use tree diagrams to organize partial derivative chains. Key concepts: tree diagrams for chain rule, implicit differentiation using partial derivatives, and applications to related rates in multiple dimensions.
