Vocabulary: chain rule, partial derivative, implicit differentiation, tree diagram, intermediate variable, dependent variable. Common errors: missing terms in the chain, confusing partial and total derivatives, and incorrect tree diagram construction.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; directional derivatives
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers directional derivatives and tangent plane approximation for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.13). The directional derivative D_u f = nabla f dot u gives the rate of change in direction u (unit vector). The tangent plane to z = f(x,y) at (a,b) is z - f(a,b) = f_x(a,b)(x-a) + f_y(a,b)(y-b). Key concepts: maximum directional derivative equals ||nabla f|| in the gradient direction, linear approximation using the tangent plane, differentials dz = f_x dx + f_y dy, and normal lines to surfaces. Vocabulary: directional derivative, gradient, tangent plane, normal line, linear approximation, differential, total differential. Common errors: using non-unit direction vectors, tangent plane formula errors, and confusing normal to surface with gradient of f(x,y).

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; coordinate transformations
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers coordinate transformations in integration for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.14). When changing variables in a multiple integral, the Jacobian determinant J = det(d(x,y)/d(u,v)) accounts for how areas/volumes scale. Key formula: double integral of f(x,y) dA = double integral of f(x(u,v), y(u,v)) |J| du dv. Key concepts: computing 2x2 and 3x3 Jacobian determinants, polar, cylindrical, and spherical Jacobians as special cases, and designing custom transformations for specific integrals. Vocabulary: Jacobian, change of variables, coordinate transformation, determinant, mapping, image, preimage. Common errors: forgetting absolute value of Jacobian, computing the inverse Jacobian, and incorrect transformation of integration bounds.

Multivariable Calculus.skill: Applications of Multiple Integrals (Volume, Mass, Center of Mass)
Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; physical applications of integration
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers physical applications of double and triple integrals for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.15). Key applications: volume = triple integral of 1 dV, mass = integral of density rho dV, center of mass coordinates (x-bar = integral of x*rho dV / mass), moments of inertia (I = integral of r^2*rho dV). Key concepts: setting up integrals with variable density, choosing appropriate coordinate systems for the geometry, and computing moments about axes and planes. Vocabulary: volume, mass, density, center of mass, centroid, moment of inertia, moment, lamina, solid. Common errors: incorrect density setup, wrong coordinate system choice, and bounds errors for irregular regions.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; potential theory
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers conservative vector fields for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.16). A vector field F is conservative if F = nabla f for some potential function f, equivalently if curl F = 0 (in simply connected domains). Key concepts: Fundamental Theorem of Line Integrals (integral of nabla f dot dr = f(B) - f(A)), path independence, finding potential functions by integration, and testing for conservativeness. Vocabulary: conservative field, potential function, path independence, exact differential, simply connected, Fundamental Theorem of Line Integrals. Common errors: assuming curl = 0 implies conservative in non-simply connected domains, integration constant errors when finding potential functions, and missing partial derivative checks.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; differential geometry
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers arc length and curvature of curves for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.17). Arc length L = integral of ||r'(t)|| dt. Curvature kappa = ||T'(t)|| / ||r'(t)|| = ||r'(t) x r''(t)|| / ||r'(t)||^3. Key concepts: unit tangent vector T = r'/||r'||, unit normal vector N = T'/||T'||, binormal vector B = T x N, Frenet-Serret formulas, radius of curvature R = 1/kappa, osculating circle and plane. Vocabulary: arc length, curvature, unit tangent, unit normal, binormal, Frenet frame, osculating circle, radius of curvature, torsion. Common errors: forgetting to normalize for unit tangent, cross product errors in curvature formula, and confusing curvature with slope.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; convergence of improper integrals
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers convergence of improper multiple integrals for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.18). Students should evaluate double and triple integrals over unbounded regions or with unbounded integrands by taking appropriate limits. Key concepts: comparison tests for convergence, converting to polar/cylindrical/spherical coordinates to simplify improper integrals, iterated limits, and recognizing when symmetry or coordinate changes resolve improper behavior. Vocabulary: improper integral, convergent, divergent, comparison test, unbounded region, unbounded integrand, limit. Common errors: assuming convergence without testing, incorrect limit setup, and not recognizing improper behavior at boundary points.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; partial differential equations
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic introduces partial differential equations (PDEs) for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.19). Students should recognize and classify common PDEs: Laplace's equation (nabla^2 u = 0), heat equation (u_t = k*nabla^2 u), and wave equation (u_tt = c^2*nabla^2 u). Key concepts: separation of variables technique, boundary conditions, initial conditions, and the Laplacian operator in different coordinate systems. Vocabulary: partial differential equation, Laplace equation, heat equation, wave equation, Laplacian, separation of variables, boundary condition, initial condition, harmonic function. Common errors: applying ODE techniques directly to PDEs, incorrect separation of variables setup, and mishandling boundary conditions.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; multivariable Taylor expansions
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers Taylor expansions in multiple variables for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.20). The second-order Taylor expansion of f(x,y) about (a,b): f(a,b) + f_x(a,b)(x-a) + f_y(a,b)(y-b) + (1/2)[f_xx(a,b)(x-a)^2 + 2f_xy(a,b)(x-a)(y-b) + f_yy(a,b)(y-b)^2]. Key concepts: linear approximation (first-order), quadratic approximation (second-order), error estimation, and connection to the Second Derivative Test for classification of critical points. Vocabulary: Taylor expansion, linear approximation, quadratic approximation, Hessian matrix, remainder, order of approximation. Common errors: missing cross-term (2f_xy), incorrect factorial denominators, and not centering the expansion properly.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; differential operators
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers differential operators for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.21). Gradient: nabla f = <f_x, f_y, f_z> (scalar to vector). Divergence: nabla dot F = P_x + Q_y + R_z (vector to scalar). Laplacian: nabla^2 f = f_xx + f_yy + f_zz (scalar to scalar). Key concepts: operator algebra (nabla^2 f = div(grad f)), physical interpretations (gradient = direction of steepest increase, divergence = source density, Laplacian = concavity in all directions), and harmonic functions (nabla^2 f = 0). Vocabulary: gradient, divergence, Laplacian, nabla, harmonic function, operator, scalar field, vector field. Common errors: confusing which operators take scalars vs. vectors, and computation errors in higher dimensions.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; differential forms theory
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers differential forms for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.22). Students should understand 0-forms (functions), 1-forms (P dx + Q dy + R dz), 2-forms, and 3-forms, and how exterior derivative d unifies gradient, curl, and divergence. Key concepts: wedge product (dx wedge dy = -dy wedge dx), exterior derivative, generalized Stokes' theorem (integral over boundary M of omega = integral over M of d*omega), and how this framework unifies Green's, Stokes', and Divergence theorems. Vocabulary: differential form, exterior derivative, wedge product, closed form, exact form, generalized Stokes' theorem. Common errors: sign errors in wedge products, confusing closed with exact forms, and applying the generalized theorem incorrectly.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; physical and engineering applications
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers physical and engineering applications of multivariable calculus for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.23). Key applications: gravitational and electromagnetic fields, fluid dynamics (continuity equation, Euler's equation), heat transfer (Fourier's law), work done by variable forces, and center of mass/moments of inertia for 3D objects. Key concepts: using vector calculus to express physical laws (Maxwell's equations, conservation laws), modeling real-world phenomena with multiple integrals, and interpreting mathematical results physically. Vocabulary: flux, circulation, work, potential energy, field, flow rate, conservation law, continuity equation. Common errors: misinterpreting physical units, incorrect coordinate system selection, and sign convention inconsistencies.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; vector calculus identities and proofs
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers fundamental identities in vector calculus for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.24). Key identities: curl(grad f) = 0, div(curl F) = 0, div(fF) = f*div(F) + F dot grad(f), curl(fF) = f*curl(F) + grad(f) x F, and the vector Laplacian. Key concepts: proving identities using component calculations, understanding why these identities hold geometrically, and using them to simplify calculations and verify results. Vocabulary: identity, curl, divergence, gradient, Laplacian, product rule, vector identity, proof. Students should be able to verify and apply these identities. Common errors: misremembering product rule analogs, component calculation mistakes, and applying identities to wrong types of fields.

Multivariable Calculus.skill: Triple Integrals in Various Coordinate Systems
Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.3(D) coordinate systems
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers evaluating triple integrals using Cartesian, cylindrical, and spherical coordinates for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.25). Students should choose the coordinate system that best matches the region's symmetry. Key volume elements: Cartesian dV = dx dy dz, cylindrical dV = r dr d(theta) dz, spherical dV = rho^2 sin(phi) d(rho) d(phi) d(theta). Key concepts: setting up integration bounds for each system, converting between systems, and recognizing which system simplifies a given problem. Vocabulary: triple integral, cylindrical coordinates, spherical coordinates, volume element, Jacobian, bounds of integration. Common errors: forgetting Jacobian factors (r for cylindrical, rho^2 sin(phi) for spherical), incorrect bound conversion, and mismatching coordinate conventions.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; surface integration and flux
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers surface integrals for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.26). Scalar surface integral: integral of f(x,y,z) dS where dS = ||r_u x r_v|| du dv. Flux integral: integral of F dot dS = integral of F dot (r_u x r_v) du dv. Key concepts: parameterizing surfaces, choosing orientation (outward normal convention for closed surfaces), computing the normal vector via cross product, and applications to fluid flow and electric flux. Vocabulary: surface integral, flux, orientation, outward normal, inward normal, parameterization, surface element. Common errors: orientation errors (affects sign of flux), incorrect parameterization, and forgetting magnitude for scalar surface integrals.

Multivariable Calculus.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.3(B) parametric/rectangular conversions
Multivariable Calculus.prompt: This topic covers representing surfaces parametrically for Multivariable Calculus students (College.MVC.27). Students should write parametric representations r(u,v) = <x(u,v), y(u,v), z(u,v)> for common surfaces and convert between parametric and implicit/explicit forms. Key parameterizations: sphere (using phi, theta), cylinder (using theta, z), cone, torus, and surfaces of revolution. Key concepts: parameter domain, tangent vectors r_u and r_v, normal vector r_u x r_v, and using parameterization for surface integrals and surface area calculations. Vocabulary: parametric surface, parameter domain, parameterization, surface of revolution, tangent vector, normal vector. Common errors: incorrect parameter ranges, ambiguous parameterization orientation, and confusing parameterizations of similar surfaces.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.5(I) solving exponential equations
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers first-order ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.1). Students should classify first-order equations (separable, linear, exact, Bernoulli) and apply the appropriate solution method. Key concepts: general vs. particular solutions, initial value problems (IVPs), direction fields for visualizing solutions, and existence/uniqueness conditions. The general first-order linear ODE dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x) is solved using an integrating factor mu(x) = e^(integral P(x)dx). Vocabulary: ordinary differential equation, order, degree, general solution, particular solution, initial condition, initial value problem. Common errors: misclassifying the equation type, forgetting the constant of integration, and not verifying solutions.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; separation of variables technique
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers separable differential equations for Differential Equations students (College.DE.2). A separable equation can be written as dy/dx = f(x)*g(y), solved by separating: integral of dy/g(y) = integral of f(x)dx + C. Key concepts: identifying separability, separating variables, integrating both sides, solving for y (when possible), and handling implicit solutions. Applications: exponential growth/decay, Newton's law of cooling, logistic growth. Vocabulary: separable equation, separation of variables, implicit solution, explicit solution, autonomous equation. Common errors: dividing by g(y) without considering g(y)=0 (singular solutions), integration errors, and forgetting absolute values in logarithms.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; linear ODE methods
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers first-order linear ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.3). Standard form: dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x). Solution method: multiply by integrating factor mu(x) = e^(integral P(x)dx), which makes the left side d/dx[mu*y]. Key concepts: identifying linear form, computing the integrating factor, recognizing that the method always works for continuous P and Q, and solving initial value problems. Vocabulary: linear ODE, integrating factor, standard form, homogeneous, nonhomogeneous, superposition. Common errors: not putting the equation in standard form first, integrating factor computation errors, and forgetting to divide by mu when solving for y.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; exact equations and integrating factor technique
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers exact differential equations for Differential Equations students (College.DE.4). The equation M(x,y)dx + N(x,y)dy = 0 is exact if dM/dy = dN/dx, meaning there exists F(x,y) such that dF = Mdx + Ndy. Key concepts: testing for exactness, finding the potential function F by integration, using integrating factors to make non-exact equations exact (mu(x) or mu(y)), and the relationship to conservative vector fields. Vocabulary: exact equation, potential function, integrating factor, total differential, conservative, test for exactness. Common errors: integration errors when finding F, forgetting to include the function of the other variable, and incorrect integrating factor determination.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; second-order ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers second-order linear ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.5). General form: y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = R(x). Key concepts: homogeneous (R=0) vs. nonhomogeneous, the superposition principle for homogeneous equations, general solution = complementary solution + particular solution, Wronskian for testing linear independence, and reduction of order when one solution is known. Vocabulary: second-order, homogeneous, nonhomogeneous, complementary solution, particular solution, Wronskian, linear independence, fundamental set. Common errors: confusing homogeneous equation with homogeneous function, Wronskian computation errors, and incomplete general solutions.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; characteristic equation method
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers constant-coefficient second-order ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.6). For ay'' + by' + cy = 0, substitute y = e^(rt) to get characteristic equation ar^2 + br + c = 0. Three cases: distinct real roots r1, r2 give y = c1*e^(r1*x) + c2*e^(r2*x); repeated root r gives y = (c1+c2*x)*e^(rx); complex roots alpha +/- beta*i give y = e^(alpha*x)*(c1*cos(beta*x)+c2*sin(beta*x)). Vocabulary: characteristic equation, auxiliary equation, distinct roots, repeated roots, complex roots, damped oscillation. Common errors: sign errors in the characteristic equation, mishandling complex roots, and forgetting the x-factor for repeated roots.

Differential Equations.skill: Nonhomogeneous Equations and Method of Undetermined Coefficients
Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; particular solution methods
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers finding particular solutions for Differential Equations students (College.DE.7). For ay'' + by' + cy = g(x), guess a particular solution y_p of the same form as g(x): polynomials for polynomial g, exponentials for exponential g, sin/cos for sinusoidal g. Key concepts: forming the correct trial function, modifying the trial when it overlaps with the complementary solution (multiply by x), and the annihilator method. Vocabulary: method of undetermined coefficients, trial solution, particular solution, complementary solution, annihilator, superposition. Common errors: wrong trial function form, forgetting to multiply by x when overlap occurs, and algebraic errors when solving for coefficients.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; variation of parameters technique
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers the variation of parameters method for Differential Equations students (College.DE.8). For y'' + P(x)y' + Q(x)y = g(x) with complementary solutions y1, y2, the particular solution is y_p = u1*y1 + u2*y2 where u1' = -y2*g/W and u2' = y1*g/W (W = Wronskian). Key concepts: this method works for any g(x) (unlike undetermined coefficients), computing the Wronskian, integrating u1' and u2', and the method extends to higher-order equations. Vocabulary: variation of parameters, Wronskian, particular solution, complementary solution, general method. Common errors: Wronskian calculation errors, integration difficulties, and sign errors in the formulas for u1' and u2'.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; integral transform methods
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers Laplace transform methods for Differential Equations students (College.DE.9). The Laplace transform L{f(t)} = F(s) = integral from 0 to infinity of e^(-st)*f(t)dt converts ODEs to algebraic equations. Key transforms: L{1} = 1/s, L{t^n} = n!/s^(n+1), L{e^(at)} = 1/(s-a), L{sin(bt)} = b/(s^2+b^2). Key concepts: linearity, first/second shift theorems, transforms of derivatives, inverse transforms using partial fractions, and solving IVPs by transforming, solving algebraically, and inverting. Vocabulary: Laplace transform, inverse transform, transfer function, shift theorem, convolution, partial fractions, s-domain. Common errors: forgetting initial conditions when transforming derivatives, partial fraction errors, and incorrect inverse transforms.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; power series solutions
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers power series solutions for Differential Equations students (College.DE.10). For equations with variable coefficients, assume y = sum a_n*x^n, substitute into the ODE, and match coefficients to find a recurrence relation. Key concepts: ordinary points vs. singular points, radius of convergence, Frobenius method for regular singular points (y = x^r * sum a_n*x^n), indicial equation, and recognizing solutions as known functions. Vocabulary: power series solution, ordinary point, singular point, regular singular point, Frobenius method, indicial equation, recurrence relation. Common errors: index shifting mistakes, incorrect recurrence relations, and not checking the radius of convergence.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; coupled ODE systems
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers coupled ODE systems for Differential Equations students (College.DE.11). Students should write systems in matrix form x' = Ax, find eigenvalues and eigenvectors of A, and construct general solutions. Key concepts: for distinct real eigenvalues x(t) = c1*v1*e^(lambda1*t) + c2*v2*e^(lambda2*t), complex eigenvalues give oscillatory solutions, repeated eigenvalues may require generalized eigenvectors, and nonhomogeneous systems x' = Ax + g(t). Vocabulary: system of ODEs, matrix form, eigenvalue, eigenvector, generalized eigenvector, fundamental matrix, matrix exponential. Common errors: eigenvector computation errors, mishandling complex eigenvalues, and forgetting generalized eigenvectors for repeated eigenvalues.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; qualitative analysis of systems
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers qualitative analysis of 2D systems for Differential Equations students (College.DE.12). Students should classify equilibrium points and sketch phase portraits. Key concepts: equilibrium points (where x' = 0), classification based on eigenvalues of the Jacobian (node, saddle, spiral, center), stability (stable if Re(lambda) < 0), nullclines, and direction field sketching. Vocabulary: phase plane, phase portrait, equilibrium point, node, saddle, spiral, center, stable, unstable, nullcline, trajectory, orbit. Common errors: misclassifying equilibrium types, confusing stable and unstable directions, and incorrect nullcline sketching.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; stability theory
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers stability theory for Differential Equations students (College.DE.13). For autonomous equations y' = f(y), equilibrium solutions occur where f(y) = 0. Key concepts: stable (nearby solutions approach), unstable (nearby solutions diverge), semistable equilibria, Lyapunov stability, linearization at equilibrium points, and the connection between eigenvalue signs and stability. Vocabulary: autonomous equation, equilibrium, stable, unstable, semistable, asymptotically stable, Lyapunov function, linearization, basin of attraction. Applications: population models, mechanical systems. Common errors: confusing stability types, linearizing incorrectly, and not considering nonlinear effects near equilibria.

Differential Equations.skill: Fourier Series and Boundary Value Problems
Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; Fourier analysis
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers Fourier analysis for Differential Equations students (College.DE.14). Students should expand periodic functions as Fourier series: f(x) = a0/2 + sum[a_n*cos(n*pi*x/L) + b_n*sin(n*pi*x/L)]. Key concepts: computing Fourier coefficients using orthogonality integrals, convergence properties, even/odd function simplifications (cosine/sine series), Gibbs phenomenon, and applying Fourier series to solve boundary value problems (heat, wave, Laplace equations). Vocabulary: Fourier series, Fourier coefficients, orthogonality, convergence, Gibbs phenomenon, boundary value problem, eigenfunction. Common errors: incorrect integration limits, forgetting the L in period adjustment, and convergence at discontinuities.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; PDEs introduction
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic introduces PDEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.15). Students should classify PDEs (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic) and solve basic PDEs using separation of variables. Key equations: heat equation u_t = k*u_xx, wave equation u_tt = c^2*u_xx, and Laplace's equation u_xx + u_yy = 0. Key concepts: boundary and initial conditions, separation of variables (assume u(x,t) = X(x)*T(t)), resulting eigenvalue problems, superposition of solutions, and physical interpretation. Vocabulary: partial differential equation, classification, separation of variables, boundary condition, initial condition, eigenvalue problem, superposition. Common errors: incorrect separation setup, missing boundary condition cases, and forgetting to superpose.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; eigenvalue methods for ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers eigenvalue methods applied to ODE systems for Differential Equations students (College.DE.16). Students should find eigenvalues (det(A-lambda*I) = 0) and eigenvectors of coefficient matrices to solve x' = Ax. Key concepts: diagonalizable systems, matrix exponential e^(At), Jordan normal form for defective matrices, and using eigendecomposition for decoupling systems. Vocabulary: eigenvalue, eigenvector, characteristic polynomial, diagonalization, matrix exponential, Jordan form, defective matrix, algebraic multiplicity, geometric multiplicity. Common errors: eigenvalue computation errors, missing eigenvectors for repeated eigenvalues, and confusing algebraic with geometric multiplicity.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; mathematical modeling with ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers mathematical modeling with ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.17). Students should derive and solve differential equation models for real-world phenomena. Key models: exponential growth/decay (dP/dt = kP), logistic growth (dP/dt = rP(1-P/K)), mixing problems (dA/dt = rate_in - rate_out), Newton's law of cooling (dT/dt = k(T-T_env)), and predator-prey (Lotka-Volterra). Vocabulary: mathematical model, growth rate, carrying capacity, logistic equation, mixing problem, Newton's cooling, predator-prey, equilibrium. Common errors: incorrect model formulation, unit inconsistencies, and misinterpreting parameters in context.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; numerical approximation methods
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers numerical ODE approximation methods for Differential Equations students (College.DE.18). Euler's method: y_{n+1} = y_n + h*f(t_n, y_n). Improved Euler (Heun's): average of slopes at start and end. Classical RK4: fourth-order Runge-Kutta with four slope evaluations per step. Key concepts: step size h affects accuracy and stability, local vs. global truncation error, order of a method, stability regions, and comparing method accuracy. Vocabulary: numerical method, Euler's method, Runge-Kutta, step size, truncation error, local error, global error, order, stability, convergence. Common errors: step size too large causing instability, accumulation of rounding errors, and misunderstanding order of accuracy.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; linearization techniques
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers linearization techniques for Differential Equations students (College.DE.19). For a nonlinear system x' = f(x,y), y' = g(x,y), linearize at equilibrium (x0,y0) using the Jacobian matrix J = [[f_x, f_y],[g_x, g_y]] evaluated at (x0,y0). Key concepts: equilibria as solutions of f=g=0, the linearized system approximates behavior near equilibria, eigenvalues of J determine local stability and type, limitations (centers in linear may be spirals in nonlinear), and Hartman-Grobman theorem. Vocabulary: linearization, Jacobian, equilibrium, hyperbolic, non-hyperbolic, Hartman-Grobman theorem, local stability. Common errors: Jacobian computation errors, applying linearization conclusions to non-hyperbolic equilibria, and confusing local with global stability.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; eigenvalue boundary value problems
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers eigenvalue boundary value problems for Differential Equations students (College.DE.20). The Sturm-Liouville problem: (p(x)y')' + (q(x) + lambda*w(x))y = 0 with boundary conditions. Key concepts: eigenvalues form an infinite sequence, eigenfunctions are orthogonal with respect to weight function w(x), eigenfunction expansions (generalized Fourier series), and self-adjoint operators. Vocabulary: Sturm-Liouville problem, eigenvalue, eigenfunction, weight function, orthogonality, self-adjoint, eigenfunction expansion, boundary condition. Applications: vibrating strings, heat conduction. Common errors: misidentifying p, q, w functions, orthogonality weight errors, and incorrect boundary condition application.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; Green's function methods
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers Green's function methods for Differential Equations students (College.DE.21). Green's function G(x,t) for L[y] = f(x) satisfies L[G] = delta(x-t) (Dirac delta). The solution is y(x) = integral of G(x,t)*f(t)dt. Key concepts: Green's function as impulse response, construction using homogeneous solutions and matching conditions (continuity, jump in derivative), connection to variation of parameters, and physical interpretation as influence function. Vocabulary: Green's function, impulse response, Dirac delta, influence function, boundary value problem, superposition integral. Common errors: incorrect matching conditions at x=t, forgetting boundary conditions for G, and confusing Green's function for different operators.

Differential Equations.skill: Applications to Mechanics and Circuit Theory
Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; physical applications of ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers physical applications of ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.22). Spring-mass systems: my'' + by' + ky = F(t) modeling free/forced, damped/undamped oscillations. RLC circuits: LQ'' + RQ' + Q/C = E(t) (charge equation). Key concepts: natural frequency, damping ratio, resonance, transient vs. steady-state solutions, impedance, and analogies between mechanical and electrical systems. Vocabulary: spring constant, damping coefficient, natural frequency, resonance, overdamped, underdamped, critically damped, transient, steady-state, impedance. Common errors: incorrect sign conventions, confusing displacement with velocity, and misidentifying resonance conditions.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; graphical ODE analysis
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers graphical ODE analysis for Differential Equations students (College.DE.23). Students should sketch direction fields (slope fields) for y' = f(x,y) by computing slopes at grid points, identify equilibrium solutions, and predict solution behavior qualitatively without solving. Key concepts: isoclines (curves where slope is constant), solution curves follow the field, autonomous equations have horizontal isoclines, and using direction fields to understand existence of solutions. Vocabulary: direction field, slope field, isocline, equilibrium solution, qualitative analysis, phase line, trajectory. Common errors: inaccurate slope calculations, not recognizing equilibrium solutions, and drawing solution curves that cross each other (violates uniqueness).

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; theoretical foundations of ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers theoretical foundations for Differential Equations students (College.DE.24). Picard's Existence and Uniqueness Theorem: if f(t,y) and df/dy are continuous in a rectangle containing (t0,y0), then y' = f(t,y), y(t0) = y0 has a unique solution in some interval. Key concepts: conditions for existence (continuity of f) vs. uniqueness (Lipschitz condition or continuity of df/dy), Picard iteration, maximal interval of existence, and examples where existence or uniqueness fails. Vocabulary: existence theorem, uniqueness theorem, Picard iteration, Lipschitz condition, maximal interval, singular solution, envelope. Common errors: assuming solutions exist globally, confusing existence with uniqueness conditions, and not checking hypotheses.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; qualitative changes in system behavior
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic introduces bifurcation theory for Differential Equations students (College.DE.25). A bifurcation occurs when a small change in a parameter causes a qualitative change in system behavior. Key types: saddle-node (two equilibria appear/disappear), transcritical (equilibria exchange stability), pitchfork (one equilibrium splits into three), and Hopf (equilibrium changes stability and limit cycle appears/disappears). Key concepts: bifurcation diagram, bifurcation parameter, structural stability, and normal forms. Vocabulary: bifurcation, bifurcation parameter, bifurcation diagram, saddle-node, transcritical, pitchfork, Hopf, limit cycle, normal form. Common errors: misclassifying bifurcation type, confusing subcritical and supercritical cases, and incomplete bifurcation diagrams.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; population modeling with ODEs
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic covers population modeling with ODEs for Differential Equations students (College.DE.26). Key models: exponential growth dP/dt = rP, logistic growth dP/dt = rP(1-P/K), Lotka-Volterra predator-prey dx/dt = ax-bxy, dy/dt = -cy+dxy, competing species, and harvesting models dP/dt = rP(1-P/K)-h. Key concepts: carrying capacity, phase plane analysis of interacting populations, equilibria and their stability, coexistence conditions, and bifurcation in harvesting models. Vocabulary: population dynamics, carrying capacity, logistic growth, predator-prey, Lotka-Volterra, competitive exclusion, coexistence, harvesting, sustainable yield. Common errors: sign errors in interaction terms, misinterpreting phase plane trajectories, and ignoring biological constraints.

Differential Equations.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; introduction to nonlinear dynamics and chaos
Differential Equations.prompt: This topic introduces chaos and nonlinear dynamics for Differential Equations students (College.DE.27). Students should understand that deterministic systems can exhibit unpredictable behavior due to sensitive dependence on initial conditions. Key concepts: nonlinear systems, limit cycles, strange attractors, Poincare maps, Lyapunov exponents (positive implies chaos), period doubling route to chaos, and the Lorenz system as a canonical example. Vocabulary: chaos, sensitive dependence, butterfly effect, strange attractor, Lorenz system, Lyapunov exponent, limit cycle, period doubling, fractal, Poincare map. Common errors: confusing randomness with chaos, assuming all nonlinear systems are chaotic, and overgeneralizing from low-dimensional examples.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on 2A.3(A) formulating systems of equations
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers solving systems of linear equations for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.1). Students should solve systems using Gaussian elimination, row reduction to echelon and reduced row echelon form, and interpret solutions geometrically. Key concepts: augmented matrix representation, elementary row operations (swap, scale, replacement), pivot positions, free variables, consistent vs. inconsistent systems, and solution types (unique, infinite with parameters, none). Vocabulary: system of equations, augmented matrix, row echelon form, RREF, pivot, free variable, consistent, inconsistent, parametric solution. Common errors: row operation arithmetic mistakes, misidentifying pivot columns, and not expressing infinite solutions with free variable parameters.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on 2A.3(B) matrix operations
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers matrix algebra for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.2). Students should perform matrix addition, scalar multiplication, matrix multiplication, and transposition. Key concepts: matrices as representations of linear transformations, matrix multiplication is not commutative (AB ≠ BA in general), dimensions must be compatible for multiplication (m×n times n×p gives m×p), identity matrix I, and zero matrix. Vocabulary: matrix, row, column, entry, transpose, identity matrix, zero matrix, square matrix, product, compatible dimensions. Common errors: multiplying incompatible matrices, assuming commutativity, and element-wise multiplication confusion.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; determinant theory and properties
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers determinant theory for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.3). Students should compute determinants using cofactor expansion, row reduction, and properties. Key properties: det(AB) = det(A)*det(B), det(A^T) = det(A), swapping rows changes sign, scaling a row scales det, det(A) = 0 iff A is singular. Key concepts: 2×2 formula (ad-bc), 3×3 cofactor expansion, connection to invertibility, volume interpretation, and Cramer's rule. Vocabulary: determinant, cofactor, minor, cofactor expansion, singular, nonsingular, invertible, Cramer's rule, signed volume. Common errors: cofactor sign pattern errors, row reduction errors affecting determinant tracking, and misapplying properties.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; abstract vector space theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers abstract vector space theory for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.4). A vector space V over a field F satisfies ten axioms (closure under addition and scalar multiplication, associativity, commutativity, distributivity, identities, inverses). Key concepts: examples beyond R^n (polynomials, matrices, functions), subspaces (must contain zero vector, be closed under addition and scalar multiplication), column space, null space, row space, and solution spaces. Vocabulary: vector space, subspace, axiom, closure, zero vector, column space, null space, row space, span. Common errors: forgetting to check the zero vector, confusing subspace with subset, and not verifying all closure conditions.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; basis and dimension concepts
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers basis and dimension concepts for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.5). A basis for a vector space V is a linearly independent spanning set. The dimension of V is the number of vectors in any basis. Key concepts: every basis of a finite-dimensional space has the same size, extending linearly independent sets to bases, reducing spanning sets to bases, coordinates relative to a basis, and standard bases for R^n, P_n, M_{m×n}. Vocabulary: basis, dimension, coordinates, standard basis, coordinate vector, finite-dimensional, infinite-dimensional. Common errors: confusing spanning sets with bases, claiming dependent sets are bases, and incorrect dimension counts for polynomial or matrix spaces.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; linear independence theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers linear independence for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.6). Vectors v1, ..., vk are linearly independent if c1*v1 + ... + ck*vk = 0 implies all ci = 0. Key concepts: testing independence by row reducing the matrix with vectors as columns, dependent sets contain a vector expressible as a linear combination of others, maximum number of independent vectors in R^n is n, and the Wronskian for function independence. Vocabulary: linearly independent, linearly dependent, trivial solution, nontrivial solution, Wronskian, span, redundant vector. Common errors: confusing independence with orthogonality, incorrect row reduction, and not checking the zero vector (which makes any set dependent).

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; linear transformation theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers linear transformations for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.7). A function T: V -> W is linear if T(u+v) = T(u)+T(v) and T(cv) = cT(v). Key concepts: every linear transformation from R^n to R^m has a unique standard matrix A (T(x) = Ax), kernel (null space of A), image (column space of A), rank = dim(image), nullity = dim(kernel), and the Rank-Nullity Theorem (rank + nullity = dim(domain)). Vocabulary: linear transformation, kernel, image, rank, nullity, standard matrix, injective (one-to-one), surjective (onto), isomorphism. Common errors: not checking both linearity conditions, confusing kernel with image, and misapplying rank-nullity.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; eigenvalue decomposition
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers eigenvalue decomposition for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.8). An eigenvalue lambda of matrix A satisfies Av = lambda*v for some nonzero eigenvector v. Key concepts: finding eigenvalues from det(A - lambda*I) = 0 (characteristic polynomial), finding eigenvectors from null space of (A - lambda*I), algebraic vs. geometric multiplicity, eigenspaces, and the characteristic polynomial. Vocabulary: eigenvalue, eigenvector, characteristic polynomial, eigenspace, algebraic multiplicity, geometric multiplicity, spectrum. Common errors: forgetting eigenvectors must be nonzero, characteristic polynomial errors, and confusing algebraic with geometric multiplicity.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; matrix diagonalization
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers matrix diagonalization for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.9). A matrix A is diagonalizable if A = PDP^(-1) where D is diagonal (eigenvalues) and P has eigenvectors as columns. Key concepts: A is diagonalizable iff it has n linearly independent eigenvectors, sufficient condition (n distinct eigenvalues), computing powers A^k = PD^kP^(-1), and applications to systems of ODEs and Markov chains. Vocabulary: diagonalizable, diagonal matrix, similar matrices, change of basis matrix, defective matrix. Common errors: assuming all matrices are diagonalizable, column order mismatch between P and D, and P^(-1) computation errors.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; inner product space theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers inner product spaces for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.10). An inner product <u,v> generalizes the dot product, satisfying positivity, symmetry (conjugate symmetry for complex), and linearity. Key concepts: the standard inner product in R^n (dot product), defining inner products on function spaces (integral of f*g), induced norm ||v|| = sqrt(<v,v>), Cauchy-Schwarz inequality |<u,v>| <= ||u||*||v||, and triangle inequality. Vocabulary: inner product, inner product space, norm, Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, triangle inequality, positive definite, Hilbert space. Common errors: not verifying inner product axioms, confusing dot product with general inner products, and conjugate symmetry errors in complex spaces.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; orthogonality concepts
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers orthogonality concepts for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.11). Vectors u and v are orthogonal if <u,v> = 0. Key concepts: orthogonal complement of a subspace W (W-perp), orthogonal projection of v onto W (proj_W(v)), orthogonal decomposition theorem (v = proj_W(v) + v_perp), orthogonal and orthonormal bases, and the projection formula proj_u(v) = (<v,u>/<u,u>)*u. Vocabulary: orthogonal, orthonormal, orthogonal complement, projection, orthogonal decomposition, perpendicular, normal. Applications: least squares, signal decomposition. Common errors: projection formula errors, confusing orthogonal with orthonormal, and not checking orthogonality of complement.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; orthonormalization algorithm
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Gram-Schmidt orthonormalization algorithm for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.12). Given linearly independent vectors {v1,...,vk}, produce orthonormal vectors {e1,...,ek}: u1 = v1, u_i = v_i - sum of proj_{u_j}(v_i), then normalize e_i = u_i/||u_i||. Key concepts: QR factorization (A = QR where Q has orthonormal columns, R is upper triangular), modified Gram-Schmidt for numerical stability, and applications to solving least squares problems. Vocabulary: Gram-Schmidt process, orthonormalization, QR factorization, orthonormal basis, projection, normalize. Common errors: projection subtraction errors, not normalizing at each step, and accumulation of numerical errors.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; least squares approximation
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers least squares approximation for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.13). When Ax = b has no exact solution, the least squares solution x-hat minimizes ||Ax - b||^2, found by solving A^T*A*x-hat = A^T*b (normal equations). Key concepts: projection interpretation (Ax-hat is the projection of b onto col(A)), connection to regression (finding best-fit line/polynomial), QR method for numerical stability, and residual analysis. Vocabulary: least squares, normal equations, projection, residual, overdetermined system, best fit, regression, pseudoinverse. Applications: data fitting, curve fitting, signal processing. Common errors: A^T*A computation errors, confusing exact solution with least squares, and not checking column rank.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; builds on P.3(G) conic sections
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers quadratic forms for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.14). A quadratic form Q(x) = x^T*A*x (A symmetric) can be classified using eigenvalues of A: positive definite (all lambda > 0), negative definite (all lambda < 0), indefinite (mixed signs). Key concepts: diagonalization of symmetric matrices, principal axis theorem (rotate coordinates to eliminate cross terms), classifying conics/quadrics using eigenvalues, and connecting to optimization (Hessian matrix). Vocabulary: quadratic form, positive definite, negative definite, indefinite, semidefinite, principal axis theorem, symmetric matrix, Hessian. Common errors: non-symmetric matrix in quadratic form, misclassifying definiteness, and eigenvalue computation errors.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; spectral decomposition theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Spectral Theorem for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.15). For real symmetric matrices: A is orthogonally diagonalizable (A = QDQ^T where Q is orthogonal and D is diagonal). Key concepts: symmetric matrices have all real eigenvalues, eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal, spectral decomposition A = sum lambda_i * u_i * u_i^T, and extension to normal matrices (A^T*A = A*A^T). Vocabulary: Spectral Theorem, orthogonal diagonalization, symmetric matrix, spectral decomposition, normal matrix, self-adjoint. Applications: principal component analysis, quantum mechanics. Common errors: applying to non-symmetric matrices, not using orthogonal eigenvector matrix, and missing the transpose relationship.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; rank-nullity theorem
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Rank-Nullity Theorem for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.16). For an m×n matrix A: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n (number of columns). Key concepts: rank = dim(column space) = dim(row space) = number of pivots, nullity = dim(null space) = number of free variables, and relationships: rank(A) = rank(A^T), rank(AB) <= min(rank(A), rank(B)). Vocabulary: rank, nullity, column space, row space, null space, pivot, free variable, dimension, full rank. Applications: determining solvability and solution structure. Common errors: confusing row rank with column rank (they're equal), misapplying to non-square matrices, and incorrect pivot counting.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; basis transformation
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers basis transformations for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.17). If B = {b1,...,bn} is a basis, the change-of-basis matrix P_B has basis vectors as columns, and [x]_B = P_B^(-1)*x gives coordinates in basis B. Matrices A and B are similar if B = P^(-1)*A*P for some invertible P. Key concepts: coordinate vectors, transition matrices between bases, similar matrices share eigenvalues, determinant, trace, and rank, and diagonalization as a special case. Vocabulary: change of basis, coordinate vector, transition matrix, similar matrices, basis, coordinate system. Common errors: direction of basis change confusion (P vs. P^(-1)), column order matters, and not verifying P is invertible.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; LA applications to ODE systems
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers using linear algebra to solve ODE systems for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.18). The system x' = Ax is solved using eigenvalues and eigenvectors: x(t) = c1*v1*e^(lambda1*t) + ... + cn*vn*e^(lambdan*t). Key concepts: matrix exponential e^(At), Jordan form for defective matrices, complex eigenvalues yielding oscillatory solutions, stability (all eigenvalues have negative real part), and phase portraits classified by eigenvalue type. Vocabulary: system of ODEs, matrix exponential, eigenvalue, eigenvector, stability, phase portrait, fundamental matrix. Common errors: eigenvector errors propagating to wrong solutions, mishandling complex eigenvalues, and stability classification mistakes.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; SVD factorization
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers SVD factorization for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.19). Every m×n matrix A can be factored as A = U*Sigma*V^T where U (m×m) and V (n×n) are orthogonal, and Sigma (m×n) is diagonal with non-negative singular values. Key concepts: singular values are square roots of eigenvalues of A^T*A, U columns are eigenvectors of AA^T, V columns are eigenvectors of A^T*A, low-rank approximation (truncated SVD), and condition number. Vocabulary: singular value decomposition, singular value, left/right singular vectors, pseudoinverse, low-rank approximation, condition number. Applications: image compression, data reduction, pseudoinverse. Common errors: ordering singular values, size mismatches, and confusing SVD with eigendecomposition.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; polar matrix factorization
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers polar matrix factorization for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.20). Every square matrix A can be written as A = UP (right polar) or A = PU (left polar) where U is orthogonal/unitary and P is positive semidefinite symmetric/Hermitian. Key concepts: P = sqrt(A^T*A) for right polar decomposition, connection to SVD (A = U*Sigma*V^T implies A = (UV^T)(V*Sigma*V^T)), geometric interpretation (rotation followed by stretching), and uniqueness conditions. Vocabulary: polar decomposition, positive semidefinite, orthogonal matrix, unitary matrix, rotation, stretching. Common errors: confusing left and right polar forms, square root of matrix computation errors, and not recognizing when P is positive definite vs. semidefinite.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; matrix factorization methods
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers LU and QR matrix factorizations for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.21). LU: A = LU where L is lower triangular and U is upper triangular (from Gaussian elimination without row swaps; with pivoting: PA = LU). QR: A = QR where Q has orthonormal columns and R is upper triangular (from Gram-Schmidt). Key concepts: LU for efficient system solving (forward/backward substitution), QR for least squares and eigenvalue algorithms, computational advantages over direct methods, and partial pivoting for numerical stability. Vocabulary: LU factorization, QR factorization, lower triangular, upper triangular, orthonormal, pivoting, forward substitution, backward substitution. Common errors: row swap handling in LU, Gram-Schmidt numerical instability, and forgetting permutation matrix in PA = LU.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; matrix polynomial identity
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers the Cayley-Hamilton Theorem for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.22). Every square matrix satisfies its own characteristic equation: if p(lambda) = det(A - lambda*I) is the characteristic polynomial, then p(A) = 0 (zero matrix). Key concepts: computing the characteristic polynomial, substituting the matrix into it, using the theorem to express A^(-1) as a polynomial in A, computing high powers of A efficiently, and minimal polynomial. Vocabulary: Cayley-Hamilton Theorem, characteristic polynomial, minimal polynomial, matrix polynomial, annihilating polynomial. Applications: computing matrix powers and inverses. Common errors: evaluating p(A) incorrectly (must replace lambda^0 with I, not 1), confusing with eigenvalue substitution, and minimal polynomial errors.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; transformation matrices in graphics
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers transformation matrices in computer graphics for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.23). Students should apply matrices for 2D and 3D transformations: rotation, scaling, reflection, shearing, and translation (using homogeneous coordinates). Key concepts: 2D rotation matrix [[cos,-sin],[sin,cos]], 3D rotation matrices about coordinate axes, homogeneous coordinates for combining affine transformations, composition of transformations via matrix multiplication, and projection matrices (orthographic and perspective). Vocabulary: transformation matrix, rotation, scaling, shearing, translation, homogeneous coordinates, affine transformation, projection, viewport, rendering pipeline. Common errors: rotation direction conventions, matrix multiplication order (right-to-left for composition), and forgetting homogeneous coordinate division.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; complex-valued vector spaces
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers complex-valued vector spaces for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.24). Students should extend real linear algebra concepts to complex scalars: complex inner product <u,v> = sum u_i*conj(v_i), Hermitian (conjugate) transpose A* = conj(A^T), unitary matrices (U*U = I), and Hermitian matrices (A* = A). Key concepts: complex eigenvalues come in conjugate pairs for real matrices, Hermitian matrices have real eigenvalues, unitary matrices preserve lengths, and spectral theorem for Hermitian matrices. Vocabulary: complex vector space, Hermitian, unitary, conjugate transpose, Hermitian transpose, sesquilinear, complex inner product. Common errors: forgetting conjugation in inner products, confusing transpose with Hermitian transpose, and applying real-only theorems to complex spaces.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; dual space theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers dual space theory for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.25). The dual space V* of a vector space V is the space of all linear functionals (linear maps from V to the scalar field). Key concepts: dual basis, natural isomorphism between V and V** (double dual) for finite-dimensional spaces, transpose of a linear transformation (T*: W* -> V* defined by T*(f) = f composed with T), annihilator of a subspace, and the relationship between rank(A) and rank(A^T). Vocabulary: dual space, linear functional, dual basis, double dual, natural isomorphism, transpose, annihilator, reflexive. Common errors: confusing dual space with the space itself, incorrect dual basis construction, and misunderstanding natural isomorphism.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; bilinear form theory
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers bilinear and quadratic forms for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.26). A bilinear form B: V×V -> F is linear in each argument: B(u,v) = u^T*A*v. A quadratic form Q(v) = B(v,v) = v^T*A*v. Key concepts: symmetric bilinear forms, matrix representation, Sylvester's law of inertia (signature is invariant under change of basis), classifying forms (positive/negative definite, semidefinite, indefinite), and polarization identity (recovering B from Q). Vocabulary: bilinear form, quadratic form, symmetric, skew-symmetric, signature, Sylvester's law, positive definite, index, congruent matrices. Common errors: confusing similar with congruent matrices, incorrect signature determination, and non-symmetric matrix handling.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; normed vector spaces
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers normed vector spaces for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.27). A norm ||v|| satisfies: positivity (||v|| >= 0, = 0 iff v = 0), homogeneity (||cv|| = |c|*||v||), and triangle inequality (||u+v|| <= ||u||+||v||). Key norms: Euclidean (L2), Manhattan (L1), max (L-infinity), and matrix norms (operator norm, Frobenius norm). Key concepts: all norms on finite-dimensional spaces are equivalent, norm induces a metric d(u,v) = ||u-v||, unit balls differ by norm, and convergence in normed spaces. Vocabulary: norm, normed space, metric, Euclidean norm, L1 norm, L-infinity norm, Frobenius norm, operator norm, unit ball, equivalence of norms. Common errors: confusing different norms, not verifying norm axioms, and applying infinite-dimensional intuitions to finite-dimensional spaces.

Linear Algebra.teks_description: College-level: Extends beyond HS TEKS; positive definiteness properties
Linear Algebra.prompt: This topic covers positive definiteness for Linear Algebra students (College.LA.28). A symmetric matrix A is positive definite if x^T*A*x > 0 for all nonzero x, equivalently all eigenvalues are positive, all leading principal minors (determinants of upper-left submatrices) are positive, and A = R^T*R for some invertible R (Cholesky decomposition). Key concepts: positive semidefinite (>= 0), tests for definiteness, Cholesky factorization A = LL^T, applications to optimization (Hessian test) and statistics (covariance matrices). Vocabulary: positive definite, positive semidefinite, Cholesky decomposition, leading principal minor, Sylvester's criterion, covariance matrix, Hessian. Common errors: not checking symmetry first, confusing positive definite with invertible, and Cholesky computation errors.
